Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 11:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Residency & retention questions for marketplace listing

Hi Caroline — thanks for the clear list. I pulled together the matrix and a short playbook for deletion; attaching both. High-level answers below and then a few action items so our teams can close the packet.

Attachments: residency_priority_matrix.xlsx (mapping by cloud/region), deletion_playbook_v1.pdf

Quick answers (marketplace default configuration unless you request an override):

- Region mapping: for marketplace listings we map storage and primary processing to the customer region group you select at onboarding. For Nebula (US customers): AWS us-east-1 will be the default object store for request payloads and model artifacts; GCP (us-west2) is used for model caching in accounts that opt for GCP runtime; Azure is only used if you specifically subscribe via an Azure marketplace SKU. We do not replicate customer data across clouds by default.

- Retention windows (defaults):
  * Raw request payloads (transient request bodies): retained 30 days unless retention extension requested.
  * Request logs & traces (for observability): retained 90 days (rotated into long-term audit store if requested).
  * Embeddings / derived feature outputs: retained 365 days by default; configurable per-account.
  * Audit logs (control-plane and admin actions): retained 365 days; can be exported to customer S3/GCS/Azure blob for longer retention.

- Deletion mechanics: automated TTL is applied per-object based on the retention policy. We also support manual purges via:
  1) Redwood Console: Account > Data > Purge (admin role required) — immediate purge request triggers background job and returns job id.
  2) Admin API: POST /v1/admin/accounts/:id/purge with reason and scope.
  3) For marketplace customers, we can schedule a documented deletion window as part of the listing (used for offboarding).

- BYOK / encryption: we support customer-managed keys. For marketplace flow:
  * AWS: support CMKs in AWS KMS (customer supplies ARN) for any S3 buckets we configure for your artifacts.
  * GCP: support Cloud KMS keys for customer projects linked through the marketplace agreement.
  * Azure: support customer-managed keys via Key Vault for marketplace SKUs where Azure resource integration is required.
  * At-rest encryption defaults to provider-managed keys if BYOK not supplied.

- Backups / replication: we do not perform cross-cloud replication of customer content for marketplace listings unless explicitly contracted (e.g., cross-region DR). Snapshots for model updater jobs are kept within the chosen cloud region; encrypted and access-controlled.

Compliance & evidence: We can provide a redacted SOC 2 package and an evidence bundle (attached). For procurement, we usually include the residency matrix + deletion playbook + SOC2 redacted report.

Action items:
- Ben: Share matrix & playbook (done — see attachments).
- Priyom: Can you confirm the API purge endpoint parameters and provide an example curl we can include in the packet?
- Marissa: Legal may need to prepare a small addendum for BYOK and explicit regional binding; can you take point?

If you need the defaults tightened (shorter retention, no caching, strict region lock), we can produce a marketplace-specific configuration and put it into the listing metadata.

Thanks — I can hop on a 30-min call if you want to run through the table.

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Partner Manager | Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0142

Hi Caroline — quick follow-up with the API example and clarification on caches.

API purge example:
curl -X POST \
  https://api.redwood.com/v1/admin/accounts/nebula-4721/purge \
  -H \"Authorization: Bearer <ADMIN_TOKEN>\" \
  -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \
  -d '{"scope":"payloads","older_than":"2027-01-01T00:00:00Z","reason":"customer-requested-offboarding"}'

Sample response (trimmed):
{
  \"job_id\": \"purge-9f2a3b\",
  \"status\": \"queued\",
  \"requested_by\": \"ben_carter@redwood.com\"
}

Notes on model cache snapshots: caches contain tokenized model state and ephemeral KV entries for session performance. We do not persist raw request payloads inside long-term model caches. If embeddings or derived features are stored, that is covered by the embeddings retention policy (365 days by default) and can be disabled. For any customer-sensitive fields we recommend applying deterministic redaction at the prompt layer prior to sending.

BYOK & audit export lead time: 3–5 business days to configure key association and storage export in our onboarding flow, plus your cloud admin granting us write permissions for the export bucket. We'll provide a checklist for your infra team.

title: Demo follow-through: evaluation blueprint & next actions
preview:
From: Rohan Mehta <rohan@redwood.ai>
To: Alicia Park <apark@stellargas.com>, Tom Nguyen <tnguyen@stellargas.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 15 Mar 2028 15:02:00 -0700
Subject: Demo follow-through: evaluation blueprint & next actions

Hi Alicia / Tom —

Thanks again for your time today — appreciated the candid discussion on where Stellargas is trying to get with conversational assistants for field ops. Quick recap from our demo and a proposed evaluation blueprint so your team can review and confirm.

What we demoed (short):
- Real-time command parsing and slot extraction from voice transcripts
- Structured output modes for downstream systems (JSON + strong typing)
- Latency contours and throughput with continuous batching enabled

Proposed success criteria (please confirm / tweak):
1) Latency: 95th percentile under 150ms for up to 512-token requests
2) Throughput: sustained 50 qps with 2x burst to 120 qps for short periods
3) Accuracy: intent+entity F1 >= 0.88 on your baseline test set
4) Integration: can write outputs to your S3 sink and call existing webhook within our auth flow

High-level POC plan (3 weeks):
- Week 0 (kickoff, 1 day): environment access, credentials, sample data handoff (owner: Rohan)
- Week 1 (ingest + smoke): baseline runs, initial tuning, validate end-to-end path (owner: Marissa)
- Week 2 (scale + regression): load tests, latency tuning, KV/cache gating, optionally try Private deployment path if needed (owner: Marissa + Ben)
- Week 3 (eval & decision): produce final metrics, short retro, next-step recommendations (owner: Rohan)

Open questions for you before we lock dates:
- Can you share a 1–2 hour sample set (or synthetic equivalent) and a preferred S3 path for ingest?
- Are there any redaction/PII requirements we need to enforce for the test data?
- Is on-prem / private deployment a hard requirement for the POC or an advanced option?

Attachments: proposed-poc-outline.pdf (PDF, 128KB) — includes timeline with checkpoints and sample eval scripts.

If this looks reasonable, can you confirm: (a) success criteria above, (b) tentative kickoff on Tue Mar 20, (c) primary technical contact for data handoff?

Thanks — I can circulate a one-page mutual action plan once you confirm.

Best,
Rohan Mehta
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
rohan@redwood.ai
mobile: +1 (415) 555-0132



From: Alicia Park <apark@stellargas.com>
To: Rohan Mehta <rohan@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tom Nguyen <tnguyen@stellargas.com>
Date: Thu, 15 Mar 2028 16:40:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Demo follow-through: evaluation blueprint & next actions

Thanks Rohan — great demo and succinct plan. A few quick clarifications from our side: 

- Success criteria tweak: throughput goal is 50 qps sustained but we need 95th percentile latency <= 150ms on payloads that average ~420 tokens (we have some longer transcripts occasionally). Can you confirm whether your numbers are with batching enabled?
- Accuracy target looks fine but we need to map your F1 to our intent taxonomy — I can share a mapping doc.
- On security: we are required to run a light security review before any data leaves our environment. Attached is our security questionnaire and a short list of must-have controls (network isolation, KMS/HSM support, audit logs).

We are interested in starting the POC. Tentative kickoff on Tue Mar 20 works; prefer an AM slot. For data handoff, Tom will own the S3 location and initial masking. Tom — can you share the S3 path and a sample file by EOD Monday?

title: Production service suppression at Lumenix — suspected quota/burst policy issue
preview:
From: Samir Khan <samir@lumenix.io>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

Hi Ava,

We're seeing a sharp drop in our throughput to Redwood (from ~800 rps to under 60 rps) starting ~08:45 PT this morning. Error rate spiked with 429s and end-to-end latency jumped from ~120ms to >1.2s. This impacted two of our customer-facing services.

Attached is a short traffic dump and a couple of example request IDs. Can your team confirm if this is on Redwood's side (rate limiting / quota / burst) or if we should look at our client-side retries? It's blocking our morning release.

Request IDs: rq_0f3b2a, rq_0f3b2b
Attachments: lumenix_traffic_snippet_20280314.csv (CSV excerpt, 2MB)

We need a short call if this is something you can resolve quickly — available 09:30 or 10:00 PT.

Thanks,
Samir
Samir Khan
Head of Platform, Lumenix
samir@lumenix.io


From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Samir Khan <samir@lumenix.io>
Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 09:35:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

Folks —

Forwarding an inbound from Lumenix. Impacting production; customer reports big spike in 429s + latency. Samir attached a short traffic dump. Can infra/SRE quickly assess whether this looks like a rate-limit/quota/burst policy enforcement or something else?

Samir — I'm pulling Karthik and Monica into the thread for immediate investigation. Marcus and Marissa are looped for visibility on escalation/credits if needed.

I can join a 09:30 PT call if that helps.

Thanks,
Ava

----Forwarded message----


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Samir Khan <samir@lumenix.io>
Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 10:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

Ava / team —

Quick triage summary after looking at Samir's dump and our control plane logs:
- Observed enforcement events from the rate-safety valve at 08:46 PT targeted at Lumenix's customer-id.
- The enforcement counter shows consecutive burst-window hits; quota window alignment appears wrong (30s burst window being interpreted as 300ms due to an off-by-factor config on the routing layer for this tenant).
- Result: continuous short-term throttling (many tokens denied) and backoff cascades on the client side leading to large perceived latency.

I've attached a config snapshot from the control plane (config_snapshot_20280314.json). Highlights:
- policy_id: psb-tenant-window-42
- burst.window_ms: 300 (expected 30000)
- enforcement_mode: permissive -> erroneously flipped to strict during a config push at 02:12 PT (suspect rollback did not complete)

Immediate recommended mitigations (short term):
1) Revert the burst.window_ms to 30000 for the tenant and reload the policy (monica).
2) Temporarily raise the tenant soft quota by 2x and allow increased burst tokens to drain backlog.
3) Enable automatic fallback routing to dedicated nodes for Lumenix for the next 4 hours (reduces policy surface).

I'll stay on the call. If you want I can run the revert and monitor.

-Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Senior Infra Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Samir Khan <samir@lumenix.io>
Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 10:25:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

On Wed, Mar 14, 2028 at 10:02 AM Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com> wrote:
> Immediate recommended mitigations (short term):
> 1) Revert the burst.window_ms to 30000 for the tenant and reload the policy (monica).
> 2) Temporarily raise the tenant soft quota by 2x and allow increased burst tokens to drain backlog.
> 3) Enable automatic fallback routing to dedicated nodes for Lumenix for the next 4 hours (reduces policy surface).

I've executed items 1 and 2 at 10:18 PT and am monitoring.
Actions taken:
- Reverted policy psb-tenant-window-42 -> burst.window_ms set to 30000 and reloaded. (config change id: cfg-20280314-019)
- Raised soft quota for tenant lumenix-cust-12 by 2x until 14:30 PT.
- Opened INF ticket: https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/INF-7123 (SRE triage + postmortem)

Early results: enforcement events dropped significantly; 429s are down to baseline in our telemetry. I'm leaving elevated quotas in place until 14:30 PT and will coordinate rollback once telemetry stabilizes.

I'll keep Samir posted in this thread.

-Monica
Monica Patel
SRE, Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Cc: Samir Khan <samir@lumenix.io>
Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 11:10:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

Team —

Thanks for the fast action. Monica/Karthik, good notes on the root cause (config drift -> off-by-factor window). From a commercial standpoint Lumenix got significant customer impact this morning.

Please prepare a short customer-facing summary (what happened, what we changed, what we will do to prevent recurrence) and let's propose a remediation package: 10% credit for the incident window + an offer for a 30-day free trial of Dedicated capacity (to avoid tenant surface policy issues). Ava, can you coordinate the wording with Samir and confirm if they want the Trial?

I'll escalate to Exec Ops for approval of the credit and note the incident in tomorrow's leadership standup. INF-7123 should capture the postmortem and follow-up action items.

-Marissa
Marissa Cole
VP, Sales Operations, Redwood Inference


From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Samir Khan <samir@lumenix.io>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 16 Mar 2028 08:43:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

Hi Samir —

Thanks again for reporting this quickly and for the sample traces. Monica reverted the burst window and we temporarily increased quota while traffic drained; telemetry shows error-rate and latency back to baseline. - 30-min wrap-up call Monday 10:00 PT to review the INF-7123 postmortem and agree on Dedicated pilot dates.

Subject: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

Samir

Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 09:35:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

Folks —

Forwarding an inbound from Lumenix. Impacting production; customer reports big spike in 429s + latency. Samir attached a short traffic dump. Can infra/SRE quickly assess whether this looks like a rate-limit/quota/burst policy enforcement or something else?

Samir — I'm pulling Karthik and Monica into the thread for immediate investigation. Marcus and Marissa are looped for visibility on escalation/credits if needed.

I can join a 09:30 PT call if that helps.

Thanks,
Ava

----Forwarded message----


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Samir Khan <samir@lumenix.io>
Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 10:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

Ava / team —

Quick triage summary after looking at Samir's dump and our control plane logs:
- Observed enforcement events from the rate-safety valve at 08:46 PT targeted at Lumenix's customer-id.
- The enforcement counter shows consecutive burst-window hits; quota window alignment appears wrong (30s burst window being interpreted as 300ms due to an off-by-factor config on the routing layer for this tenant).
- Result: continuous short-term throttling (many tokens denied) and backoff cascades on the client side leading to large perceived latency.

I've attached a config snapshot from the control plane (config_snapshot_20280314.json). Highlights:
- policy_id: psb-tenant-window-42
- burst.window_ms: 300 (expected 30000)
- enforcement_mode: permissive -> erroneously flipped to strict during a config push at 02:12 PT (suspect rollback did not complete)

Immediate recommended mitigations (short term):
1) Revert the burst.window_ms to 30000 for the tenant and reload the policy (monica).
2) Temporarily raise the tenant soft quota by 2x and allow increased burst tokens to drain backlog.
3) Enable automatic fallback routing to dedicated nodes for Lumenix for the next 4 hours (reduces policy surface).

I'll stay on the call. If you want I can run the revert and monitor.

-Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Senior Infra Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Samir Khan <samir@lumenix.io>
Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 10:25:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Sudden throughput drop, lots of 429s — urgent

On Wed, Mar 14, 2028 at 10:02 AM Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com> wrote:
> Immediate recommended mitigations (short term):
> 1) Revert the burst.window_ms to 30000 for the tenant and reload the policy (monica).
> 2) Temporarily raise the tenant soft quota by 2x and allow increased burst tokens to drain backlog.
> 3) Enable automatic fallback routing to dedicated nodes for Lumenix for the next 4 hours (reduces policy surface).

I've executed items 1 and 2 at 10:18 PT and am monitoring.
Actions taken:
- Reverted policy psb-tenant-window-42 -> burst.window_ms set to 30000 and reloaded. (config change id: cfg-20280314-019)
- Raised soft quota for tenant lumenix-cust-12 by 2x until 14:30 PT.
- Opened INF ticket: https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/INF-7123 (SRE triage + postmortem)

Early results: enforcement events dropped significantly; 429s are down to baseline in our telemetry. I'm leaving elevated quotas in place until 14:30 PT and will coordinate rollback once telemetry stabilizes.

I'll keep Samir posted in this thread.

-Monica
Monica Patel
SRE, Redwood Inference

Please prepare a short customer-facing summary (what happened, what we changed, what we will do to prevent recurrence) and let's propose a remediation package: 10% credit for the incident window + an offer for a 30-day free trial of Dedicated capacity (to avoid tenant surface policy issues). Ava, can you coordinate the wording with Samir and confirm if they want the Trial?

I'll escalate to Exec Ops for approval of the credit and note the incident in tomorrow's leadership standup. INF-7123 should capture the postmortem and follow-up action items.

-Marissa

title: SLO traffic-shaping + connection quotas — post-call recap
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Moore <alex.moore@skybound.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 14:05:00 -0700
Subject: SLO traffic-shaping + connection quotas — post-call recap

Alex — thanks again for the call earlier. Quick written recap so we have a clear set of numbers to work from and a proposed next-step path.

Call highlights (from our side):
- Skybound: real-time transcription + intent extraction pipeline; mostly streaming audio -> tokens -> inference.
- Target SLOs: p95 latency for inference under streaming at ~200ms (customer-facing).
- Current traffic profile: pronounced diurnal peaks and frequent short bursts during morning and evening UX spikes.

What we need from you (to finalize a quote and quota proposal):
1) Typical concurrent sessions during peak (how many simultaneous user streams active?).
2) Average request rate per session (tokens/sec or inference calls/sec) while streaming.
3) Expected peak burst size and typical burst duration (e.g., 10k reqs for 30s).
4) Any hard limits on p99 latency or tail-latency SLAs beyond p95.

What we can provisionally offer (high-level):
- Hosted API: adjustable connection pool (account-level) + per-minute QPS quota. Default hosted tiers have a 1k concurrent connection baseline; we can propose an increased pool for Skybound to match expected concurrency.
- Burst windows: we support short-term burst allowance (automatic smoothing + token-bucket style enforcement) — helpful for UX spikes.
- Streaming optimizations: prefix/KV caching and continuous-batching help reduce per-token latency on long-lived streams.

Attachments: I attached a small template (conn_quota_template.xlsx) you can fill with sample week-of traffic (timestamps, concurrent sessions, avg rps per session) — that lets us simulate cost and select a recommended quota.

Proposed next steps:
- You paste numbers into the template and return — we will run an internal sim and send 2 options (hosted with raised quotas, or Dedicated reserved capacity with guaranteed concurrency).
- Schedule a technical deep-dive with Priyom (runtime/ops) to discuss connection pooling, socket multiplexing, and fallback behavior.

Thanks — happy to jump on a quick follow if helpful.

—Ben
Ben Carter | Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0192



From: Alex Moore <alex.moore@skybound.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>, Samir Patel <samir@skybound.ai>
Date: Wed, 14 Mar 2028 16:33:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SLO traffic-shaping + connection quotas — post-call recap

Ben — thanks for the clear recap and template. I filled out the high-level numbers below and attached a sample traffic export (skybound_traffic_profile.csv).

Quick answers:
- Typical concurrent sessions (peak week): 1,200 active streams.
- Average inference calls per session while active: ~2.5 calls/sec (short inference chunks ~200–400 tokens).
- Peak bursts: we see up to 6,000 requests/sec sustained for ~45–90s during promo flushes.
- Tail concerns: p99 under heavy bursts is important; our internal SLAs say p99 < 800ms for business-critical flows. p95 target of 200–250ms.
- We will need server-initiated stream closure handling (graceful close) and an approach for reconnect backoff so UX doesn't hammer the backend.

Two questions for Redwood:
1) If we stay hosted, what is your suggested account-level concurrent connection pool and burst allowance to meet p95=200ms for our profile?
2) What are the rough uplift % and commitment tiers for Dedicated to guarantee p99 < 800ms during peak bursts?

We are open to a small PoC (7–14 days) running a subset of traffic (non-production) to validate latency under bursts. Ideally want: 1) rough quote, 2) PoC plan, 3) timeline for enabling increased connection pool on hosted account.

Alex
Alex Moore | Head of Platform, Skybound AI
alex.moore@skybound.ai | +1 (650) 555-0148

Attachment: skybound_traffic_profile.csv


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Moore <alex.moore@skybound.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 15 Mar 2028 08:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SLO traffic-shaping + connection quotas — post-call recap

Alex — appreciate the numbers and the sample. We ran a quick back-of-envelope sim with Priyom and here are two pragmatic options to discuss with your infra team: 

Option A — Hosted (fastest time-to-value):
- Provisioning: increase account connection pool to 3,000 concurrent connections.
- Burst policy: token-bucket allowing bursts up to 10,000 requests/sec for short windows (max 90s) with automatic smoothing; sustained QPS above baseline will have throttling options/302 backpressure behavior.
- Expected latency: with our streaming optimizations and the token rates you provided, p95 ~180–250ms is achievable for most bursts under 6k sustained req/s; p99 may creep toward ~700–900ms at the extreme end of 6k+ sustained unless we enable dedicated resources.
- Pricing: uplift on hosted per-token + small monthly quota surcharge for increased connection pool (I can share numbers if you want a line-item).

Option B — Dedicated (reserved capacity):
- Provisioning: reserved GPU pool sized for your peak profile; can guarantee node reservations tuned for streaming concurrency.
- Concurrency guarantee: target 6k+ concurrent inference workers with headroom for bursts; can be sized to enforce p99 < 800ms under agreed traffic envelope.
- Commitment: monthly reserved commit (we'll propose a 6–12 month starting point).
- Cost: higher fixed cost but predictable unit economics and stronger latency SLAs.

PoC suggestion:
- 7–14 day PoC on Hosted with raised connection pool (Option A) followed by short dedicated-sim run to compare tail behavior. We can use a canary tagging approach (10% traffic) to limit blast radius.

Next steps I propose:
1) Priyom to run a quick config simulation on your CSV and return a more precise token/QPS -> latency projection. (Priyom, can you take this today?)
2) If numbers look good, we draft a PoC runbook and put a HubSpot deal note (I linked the deal in this thread).
3) Technical deep-dive to discuss socket multiplexing and strategy for reconnect/backoff — Marissa, can you propose 2 slots tomorrow?

Thanks — will follow up with a short quote table once Priyom posts the sim.

—Ben
Ben Carter | Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>, Alex Moore <alex.moore@skybound.ai>
Date: Thu, 15 Mar 2028 09:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SLO traffic-shaping + connection quotas — post-call recap

Looping in a couple of notes from the pricing side and to lock scheduling.

Ben — please include two price alternatives in the quote: 1) Hosted raised-quota monthly estimate (with expected overage rules), and 2) Dedicated reserved option (6 month and 12 month commit tiers). Call out the PoC price (discounted) and whether we can provide a one-off burst credit for the initial test.

Alex — are mornings PST at 10:00 or 11:00 ok for a 45-minute technical deep-dive tomorrow? We will have Priyom, myself (pricing + partnerships), and one runtime engineer on the call. Please confirm preferred slot and we will send calendar invite + an agenda.

On reconnect/backoff: Priyom — please prepare recommended client-side backoff (initial retry/backoff windows, jitter, and recommended reconnect throttling) and a short note on how our connection pool behaves under TCP churn.

Thanks — Marissa
Marissa Cole | Director, Strategic Accounts, Redwood Inference

Quick answers:

- Average inference calls per session while active: ~2.5 calls/sec (short inference chunks ~200–400 tokens).
- Peak bursts: we see up to 6,000 requests/sec sustained for ~45–90s during promo flushes.

Alex — appreciate the numbers and the sample. We ran a quick back-of-envelope sim with Priyom and here are two pragmatic options to discuss with your infra team: 

Option A — Hosted (fastest time-to-value):
- Provisioning: increase account connection pool to 3,000 concurrent connections.
- Burst policy: token-bucket allowing bursts up to 10,000 requests/sec for short windows (max 90s) with automatic smoothing; sustained QPS above baseline will have throttling options/302 backpressure behavior.
- Expected latency: with our streaming optimizations and the token rates you provided, p95 ~180–250ms is achievable for most bursts under 6k sustained req/s; p99 may creep toward ~700–900ms at the extreme end of 6k+ sustained unless we enable dedicated resources.
- Pricing: uplift on hosted per-token + small monthly quota surcharge for increased connection pool (I can share numbers if you want a line-item).

Option B — Dedicated (reserved capacity):
- Provisioning: reserved GPU pool sized for your peak profile; can guarantee node reservations tuned for streaming concurrency.
- Concurrency guarantee: target 6k+ concurrent inference workers with headroom for bursts; can be sized to enforce p99 < 800ms under agreed traffic envelope.
- Commitment: monthly reserved commit (we'll propose a 6–12 month starting point).
- Cost: higher fixed cost but predictable unit economics and stronger latency SLAs.

PoC suggestion:
- 7–14 day PoC on Hosted with raised connection pool (Option A) followed by short dedicated-sim run to compare tail behavior. We can use a canary tagging approach (10% traffic) to limit blast radius.

title: Request: Temporary SLO exemption for MedSummarizer-v2 (preview)
preview:
From: Asha Patel <asha.patel@stratahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@stratahealth.com, security@stratahealth.com
Date: 2028-03-11T09:02:00-07:00
Subject: Request: temporary SLO relief for MedSummarizer-v2-preview

Hi Ben —

Thanks for the demo last week. We kicked off a small internal pilot of MedSummarizer-v2 (preview) for discharge summarization. It is promising, but we're seeing intermittent higher tail latency (~P95-P99) when feeding full encounter contexts (20k+ tokens). That matters for our physician UX. Security and procurement asked whether Redwood can provide: 

- A temporary allowance (30–45 days) relaxing the 99.9% latency SLO to 99.0% for the preview model OR a documented fallback routing plan so our app never times out.
- A written plan for Canary routing or dedicated pool during the pilot window.
- Any credits/fee offsets for degraded latency during the preview.

I've attached a small sample of tail-latency observations (latency-sample.csv) and copied procurement/security who will need the formal paperwork. Please let me know what you need from our side (example prompts, sample conversation IDs, legal contacts).

Best,
Asha Patel
Head of Integrations, StrataHealth
asha.patel@stratahealth.com
Attachment: latency-sample.csv (csv)


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-03-11T09:24:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Request: temporary SLO relief for MedSummarizer-v2-preview

Team — forwarding request from StrataHealth. TL;DR: pilot sees P99 spikes with very long contexts. They want a 30–45 day temporary SLO relaxation OR guaranteed fallback routing. They also asked about credits.

Questions for each of you:
- Monica: commercial approach (credits, contract addendum) and procurement contacts?
- Vivek: can infra offer a dedicated pool / canary routing and what's the realistic P99 impact with mitigations (k-v cache, batching)?
- Grace: product risk and rollback triggers we should propose (telemetry thresholds, error-rate limits).

Attaching our SLA-Exception-Request-Form for initial capture. I want a proposed playbook by EOD tomorrow so I can loop legal/procurement.

Thanks,
Ben
Senior Account Exec, Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com
Attachment: SLA-Exception-Request-Form.pdf (pdf)

> Hi Ben —
> 
> Thanks for the demo last week. We kicked off a small internal pilot...


From: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-03-11T14:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Request: temporary SLO relief for MedSummarizer-v2-preview

Ben / team —

Thanks for the ping. I filled out the attached form with initial commercial suggestions and a proposed 30-day carveout (2028-03-20 to 2028-04-19) with the following conditions: 

1) Dedicated small GPU pool for pilot (N=2 instances, M-64 equivalent) to reduce noisy neighbor variance.
2) Canary routing for 10% of traffic on MedSummarizer-v2-preview, with automatic rollback if error-rate > 1% or P99 exceeds 6s for 24h straight.
3) 20% invoice credit applied to billed inference tokens for the carveout window if we cannot meet baseline SLO outside agreed fallback windows.
4) Security completes checklist (I put a short set of items for their review).

I've attached the filled PDF for legal/procurement to start redlines: slo-exception-form-filled.pdf. Questions for security/procurement (feel free to forward to them): what legal language do you need for temporary SLO adjustments? Does Strata require an explicit amendment or is a signed form sufficient?

If approved, we should schedule a short sync with Strata to confirm telemetry signals and the canary rollout cadence.

Monica
Sales Operations, Redwood Inference
Attachment: slo-exception-form-filled.pdf (pdf)


From: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-03-12T08:11:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Request: temporary SLO relief for MedSummarizer-v2-preview

Quick infra readout after looking at their sample:

- Cause: long context lengths (18–24k tokens) push past our optimized KV cache heuristics; we see increased memory churn and a heavier attention kernel path that amplifies tail latency.
- Mitigations: dedicated pool will help (isolates scheduling/GC). We can enable aggressive prefix caching for their session IDs and bias smaller batch windows for that pool.
- Expected impact: with dedicated pool + cache tuning I estimate median ~unchanged, P95 small increase (~10–15%), P99 could still be 30–40% higher than our standard SLO in the first 48–72 hours as caches warm and telemetry matures.
- Canary: we can do a 10% canary on a dedicated pool with tracing hooks and a fallback to our older stable model (MedSummarizer-v1) on a per-request timeout (3s). That fallback will preserve UX for physicians.
- Resource ask: reserve two x86 GPU nodes (M-64 equivalent) for 30 days; cost delta estimate attached.

I'll open INF-4820 with runbook steps and dashboards (link below). If product/legal wants rollback triggers, we'll implement automated routing switches keyed off P99 and error-rate.

Vivek
Engineering Lead, Serving Runtime
vivek_kulkarni@redwood.com
Attachment: infra-cost-estimate.pdf (pdf)
Link: https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/INF-4820


From: Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-03-13T10:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Request: temporary SLO relief for MedSummarizer-v2-preview

Thanks — this plan looks reasonable to me from a product perspective, provided we codify clear telemetry and rollback thresholds. Proposed additional guardrails: 

- Canary telemetry: track P50/P95/P99, request timeouts, and payload-size distribution. Canary fails if any of these hit their thresholds for two consecutive 6h windows.
- Logging/PII: ensure that Strata confirms redaction for any PHI sent to preview model or that they agree to use our private deployment path (they've said pilot is in-cloud). We must have Security sign off on data handling.
- Escalation: if fallback to v1 is used >5% of requests for 48h, we automatically pause the carveout and notify account leadership + legal.

Once Security signs the filled form and legal approves the doc language, I'll OK the canary schedule.

Grace
Product Manager, Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Asha Patel <asha.patel@stratahealth.com>, procurement@stratahealth.com
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>, security@stratahealth.com
Date: 2028-03-14T16:27:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Request: temporary SLO relief for MedSummarizer-v2-preview — proposed carveout and next steps

Asha — thanks for the sample data and patience while we scoped this. Proposed carveout summary below (short form):

- Window: 2028-03-20 to 2028-04-18 (30 days)
- Infrastructure: dedicated pilot pool (2 M-64 equiv nodes) + 10% canary routing, fallback to MedSummarizer-v1 at 3s per-request timeout.
- Telemetry & rollback: automatic rollback triggers if P99 > 6s for a rolling 24h or if fallback usage >5% for 48h. Grace to own telemetry thresholds.
- Credits: 20% credit applied to inference usage for the carveout window if we fail to meet the fallback guarantees.
- Security: Redwood will provide a short checklist for data handling and we will pursue either a signed form or a brief amendment — whichever your legal prefers.

Attached: slo-exception-form-filled.pdf (for signature), a short runbook link, and the HubSpot deal for your procurement team. If this looks good I can get legal to send a one-page amendment/DocuSign and we can schedule the canary launch on 2028-03-21.

Next steps:
1) Procurement/legal: confirm amendment vs signed form and advise on signers.
2) Strata security: confirm PHI handling choice (redaction or private deployment).
3) Redwood infra: reserve nodes and enable tracing hooks (Vivek).

Open items for us internally: final cost delta and ownership of credit accounting (Monica).

Ben
Senior Account Exec, Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com
Attachments: slo-exception-form-filled.pdf, infra-cost-estimate.pdf
Link: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/1234567/deal/SH-2028-0042

title: [Cedarline] On‑prem cluster: sudden CPU throttling + 503s — immediate tuning request
preview:
From: Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@cedarlinehealth.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Cc: ops@cedarlinehealth.com
Date: Sat, 11 Mar 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Production: on‑prem cluster p95 spike + 503s — immediate help needed

Hi Camila / Marcus,

We started seeing a sudden jump in p95 latency and intermittent 503s on our Cedarline on‑prem cluster around 08:50 PT. Key symptoms: sustained CPU steal on the inference nodes, NVidia GPU utilization is high but token throughput dropped, and the worker processes started returning 503s for short windows (2–3 minutes) every ~10 minutes.

Attached is a short trace and sample nginx/worker logs (trace-20280311.log). We haven't changed traffic patterns or model versions in the last 48h. This is impacting multiple product teams — p95 moved from ~420ms to ~1.8s and error rate climbed to 2.1% on production route /api/v1/generate.

Can Redwood confirm whether any kernel/cgroups changes on the host side could cause CPU throttling observed here? We need an immediate mitigation plan and, if possible, a low-risk tuning recommendation we can apply during a 30‑minute maintenance window.

Tom
Ops Lead, Cedarline Health

Attachments: trace-20280311.log (text), nginx-sample.log (text)


From: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>
To: Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@cedarlinehealth.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 11 Mar 2028 09:28:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Production: on‑prem cluster p95 spike + 503s — immediate help needed

Tom — thanks for the quick alert and the logs. I’m looping in Marcus (serving runtime) and Hannah (site reliability) to triage. Markus (cto) and Laura (customer success) are on notice; I’ll keep you posted on mitigation and timeline.

Initial asks from our side:
- Marcus/Hannah: can you confirm whether host kernel OOM/kthrottling/cgroups events correlate with the timestamps in the trace?
- Tom: if you can share the last 24h of host sysstat and dmesg snippets that would help.

We may propose a temporary change (cgroups cpu.shares / CPU affinity pinning or reduced batch target) that you can apply during a short maintenance window — will advise shortly.

— Camila
Account Exec, Redwood Inference



From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>, Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@cedarlinehealth.com>
Cc: Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 11 Mar 2028 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Production: on‑prem cluster p95 spike + 503s — immediate help needed

Tom/Camila — quick technical read: I looked at the attached trace. Two likely contributing factors surfaced: 

1) Host CPU contention: trace shows consistent softirq pressure and CPU steal on cores that are pinned to inference processes. It looks like a noisy co‑tenant process (backup/cron) triggered a spike in system CPU consumption at ~08:49 and again at ~09:03.
2) KV cache churn + large prefix cache misses: memory pressure on the host caused our K/V cache to evict aggressively, which increased decode latency due to repeated KV reloads.

Short mitigation suggestions (low risk):
- Pause non‑critical host cron/backup jobs on the inference nodes for 1 hour.
- Apply stricter CPU cgroup isolations for inference containers: reduce cpu.shares for background jobs and set cpu.cfs_quota_us for batch workers.
- Temporarily lower target batch size from 64 to 32 and reduce batching window from 60ms to 30ms to avoid long tail queueing.

I can provide a quick tuning diff and commands to run. If we still see 503s after these, we should consider kernel irqbalance adjustments and pinning the inference worker threads to GPUs with numactl.

— Marcus
Senior Software Engineer, Serving Runtime

Attachment: kvcache-config.diff (patch snippet)


From: Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@cedarlinehealth.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 11 Mar 2028 12:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Production: on‑prem cluster p95 spike + 503s — immediate help needed

On Sat, Mar 11, 2028 at 10:05 AM Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com> wrote:
> Tom/Camila — quick technical read: I looked at the attached trace. Two likely contributing factors surfaced:
> 
> 1) Host CPU contention...

Ran quick health checks against the fleet and pulled dmesg/sysstat for the interval. Confirm Marcus’ findings: there were two cron jobs (logrotate + nightly metadata sync) that started at the problematic times and consumed ~35–40% of CPU on the host. We also saw repeated vmstat involuntary context switches and elevated iowait.

My proposed immediate plan (for Cedarline ops to run during a 30‑minute maintenance window):
1) Disable the two identified cron jobs on the inference hosts (commands provided below).
2) Apply the cpu.shares / cpu.cfs_quota_us tweaks Marcus suggested. Example commands attached in tuning-proposal-cedarline.md.
3) Push a light KV cache warmup script to reduce aggressive eviction (one‑line python script included in the md).

If Cedarline approves, I recommend a canary on one rack for 30 minutes; if stable, roll to remaining racks. I’m attaching a short tuning proposal with exact commands and rollback steps.

Hannah
SRE Lead, Redwood Inference

Attachments: tuning-proposal-cedarline.md (text)


From: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>
To: Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@cedarlinehealth.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 11 Mar 2028 13:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Production: on‑prem cluster p95 spike + 503s — immediate help needed

Tom — thanks for confirming we can schedule a 30‑minute window starting at 14:00 PT.

Planned steps (summary):
- Canary on rack‑03 (one node) at 14:00: disable cron jobs, apply cpu cgroup tweaks, drop batch target to 32, start KV warmup.
- Monitor p95 and error rate for 30 minutes. If p95 < 700ms and errors < 0.5% we will roll.
- Rollback plan: revert cgroup changes and re‑enable cron if we observe regressions.

I’ve attached the tuning proposal (Hannah) and Marcus’ kvcache patch. Laura will own customer comms. Markus, please flag if you want an exec briefing after the canary.

— Camila
Account Exec, Redwood Inference



From: Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwood.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.com>, Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@cedarlinehealth.com>
Date: Mon, 13 Mar 2028 14:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Post‑canary summary & ask: executive note for leadership review

Camila / Laura — brief exec summary for our leadership sync: 

Outcome: Canary executed 2028‑03‑11 14:00 PT on rack‑03. Immediate results: p95 dropped from ~1.8s to ~560ms within 7 minutes after applying cgroup and batching changes; error rate fell from 2.1% to 0.2%. KV warmup reduced cache misses by ~65% in 20 minutes. No regressions observed in downstream services.

Root cause (concise): Host CPU contention from scheduled host cron jobs caused system softirq/iowait spikes that, combined with KV cache churn, produced queuing and intermittent 503s.

Request: For leadership — approve the following medium‑risk persistent changes for Cedarline production hosts:
- Persist cpu.cfs_quota_us limits for inference containers and move background jobs to dedicated maintenance windows.
- Add a small KV warmup job at service startup and increase host RAM reservation for cache by 6–8%.
- Schedule a follow‑up kernel tuning pass (irqbalance, NOHZ settings) with our infra team; estimate 3–4 engineering hours.

Customer COMMS: Laura, Laura please confirm language for the executive note to Cedarline leadership (Tom ECC and Director of Engineering). I recommend offering a 2‑week post‑tuning support window and a credit for the incident.

Markus
CTO, Redwood Inference

title: Connector sourcing briefing and P3 partner ask
preview:
From: Sam Reed <sam@pinecone.io>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: partners@redwood-partners.example.com
Date: Fri, 10 Mar 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Intro: connector sourcing for vector storage

Hi Avery / team,

Thanks for taking a quick look — we had a brief internal sync at Pinecone about your connector requirements sheet and wanted to kick off a lightweight intake. Goal: align on the minimal technical checklist and the preferred commercial path (co-sell vs. marketplace listing vs. integration referral).

Quick context: we're focusing on a hardened connector + cert workflow that covers:
- index lifecycle (create/delete/replicate)
- auth patterns (service tokens, ephemeral creds)
- fallthrough to S3/pgvector for cold tiers
- observability hooks (request-level tracing, error codes)

Would love a 45m intake next week with engineering + partner ops. I attached a short worksheet (connector_requirements_v1.xlsx) showing the things we commonly need.

Proposed times: Tue 3/14 9–10am PT or Wed 3/15 11–11:45am PT.

Cheers,
Sam Reed
Partner Integrations, Pinecone

title: Interlock: clarify roadmap gaps + QBR prioritization
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sarah Li <sarah.li@acmehealth.com>, procurement@acmehealth.com
Date: 2028-03-07T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Interlock: clarify roadmap gaps + QBR prioritization

Team —

Thanks for joining the sync this morning. Quick recap and asks so we can finalize the QBR pack for Acme next week. Main points to land:

- Acme has flagged two production exceptions they want addressed before enterprise review: latency regression on longer contexts (+15% p95) and a data retention clarification for Dedicated mode. See attached security_exception_log.csv.
- They want a clear prioritization for the QBR: 1) SLA/latency mitigation plan, 2) procurement path (contracting timeline), 3) feature roadmap asks (KV caching improvements, adaptive batching).

Action asks (please reply with owners & ETA by EOD Wednesday):
- Vivek: technical mitigation proposal for p95 regression, include short/medium term moves and expected delta.
- Karthik: realistic ETA for KV cache rollout vs. scope reduction options.
- Marissa: lead the commercial thread on procurement timeline & any ask for credits / SLA exceptions.
- Naomi: consolidate QBR deck bullets and a one-slide exec ask (we need an explicit ask for the QBR).

Attachments: QBR_prep_template.xlsx, security_exception_log.csv. I uploaded the working folder here: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3QBR-Prep-Docs

Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise AE, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sarah Li <sarah.li@acmehealth.com>, procurement@acmehealth.com
Date: 2028-03-07T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Interlock: clarify roadmap gaps + QBR prioritization

Thanks — quick updates from my side:

- Commercial / procurement: Acme wants an answer on contract term & a sample SOW by 3/16. Procurement also requested a standard SOC 2 addendum and an SLA credit discussion if p95 is not met for two consecutive weeks. I will coordinate a redline window — expect the initial DocuSign by 3/12.
- Exec ask suggestion: request a 30% committed reserved capacity pilot for 60 days to stabilize latency while we push the fixes (this is the ask Naomi should put on the slide).

Open question: do we have a fallback model variant that maintains throughput under long-context loads? If yes, can we offer a temporary routing policy to avoid user-visible regressions?

Marissa
Marissa Cole
Director, Customer Success, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2028-03-08T08:26:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Interlock: clarify roadmap gaps + QBR prioritization

Ben / team —

Short technical reality: the KV caching improvements are on the roadmap but delivering full adaptive cache eviction across all models will slip into Q3 unless we scope an MVP that ships in 6 weeks. Two options:

1) MVP scope (6 weeks): prefix caching + TTL-based eviction for top 3 enterprise models. Expected p95 improvement: ~9–12% for long-context workloads.
2) Full rollout (Q3): adaptive eviction + routing policies; expected p95 improvement: 18–22% but longer lead time.

I can draft a short doc with the tradeoffs. Drive note: I added a short runbook sketch to the shared folder (see drive link).

Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Sr. Director, Serving Runtime
karthik_iyer@redwoodinference.com


From: Sarah Li <sarah.li@acmehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: procurement@acmehealth.com
Date: 2028-03-08T14:51:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Interlock: clarify roadmap gaps + QBR prioritization

Thanks for the clear asks. A few points from Acme procurement and engineering:

- Procurement asks for a documented timeline for SOC 2 addendum and the sample SOW. Can you confirm dates?
- Engineering would prefer option (1) MVP if it can be delivered in 6 weeks. They are worried about committing to a bigger change before seeing measurable gains.
- Regarding credits: procurement wants to understand our fallback options before discussing credits. If you can propose a temporary routing policy we can accept that over credits for now.

We appreciate the drive link & will sync with you after you share the mitigation doc.

—Sarah
Sarah Li
Director of Platform Engineering, Acme Health
sarah.li@acmehealth.com

- Commercial path: Marissa to circulate the sample SOW and the initial DocuSign by 3/12; procurement and Sarah to confirm contract window.
- Exec ask: Naomi to produce a single-slide ask recommending a 60-day reserved pilot and what success looks like (p95 target, traffic cap). Slide due 3/13 morning.

Open items: we still need a one-paragraph fallback policy we can share with procurement (temp routing to compatible variant). Karthik / Vivek please include that in the mitigation doc.

title: Aligning cost/perf knobs: owner map and implementation checklist
preview:
From: Sofia Vega <svega@atlasai.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 09 Mar 2028 10:12:00 -0700
Subject: Cost/perf knobs before prod rollout - need owner map and checklist

Hi Ava / Rishi,

Thanks again for the demo last week — AtlasAI is excited to move to a dedicated deployment with Redwood. Before we get into procurement, we need a concrete implementation checklist covering the cost/perf levers we discussed: batching, KV/prefix caching, quantization profile options, and routing policies (region vs. cost tiers).

Two asks from our side to keep this tight for our internal steering committee: 
- A short owner map (who at Redwood will own each knob during implementation and who we should ping for runbook questions).
- A prioritized checklist with expected unit-economics impact and rough effort (small/medium/large).

Constraints to note: we need 95th %-ile request latency under 400ms for our chat front-end at 10k daily active users, and AtlasAI requires VPC/private control plane. Also attaching our security survey (atlasai-security-survey.pdf) for reference.

Can you send a proposed owner map and a short checklist by EOD Monday? That will let us schedule the implementation kickoff.

Best,
Sofia Vega
Head of Platform Engineering
AtlasAI
(attachment: atlasai-security-survey.pdf, type: pdf)

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Sofia Vega <svega@atlasai.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 09 Mar 2028 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Cost/perf knobs before prod rollout - need owner map and checklist

Hi Sofia — thanks for the clear asks and for the survey.

We can definitely put together an owner map + prioritized checklist. Tentative owners internally are:
- Batching & autoscaling cadence: Ava Chen (me) — design/ops + tuning playbook
- KV/prefix caching and prefetch heuristics: Karthik Iyer — cache sizing and TTLs
- Quantization profile & evals: Rishi Malhotra — accuracy regressions and profile selection
- Smart routing (region/cost fallback): Karthik + infra team (cc'd) — policy config and fallbacks

I've attached a compact owner map and a short playbook focused on prefetch/batching heuristics: econ-knobs-owner-map.xlsx and prefetch-batching-playbook.pdf. The owner map includes expected impact buckets (token-cost, latency, reliability) and an initial effort estimate.

Proposed short schedule:
1) Monday (Mar 12) — 1-hour kickoff: alignment and priorities
2) Week 1 — baseline load & seq-length distribution capture (we'll provide a small collection script)
3) Week 2 — run quantization evals on a dev fork + caching baseline
4) Week 3 — canary rollout with routing policy and autoscale thresholds

If that works I can send a meeting invite and a one-pager with pre-work items for AtlasAI (sample logs, expected seq-lengths, representative prompt set).

Thanks —
Ava Chen
Customer Success, Redwood Inference
(attachment: econ-knobs-owner-map.xlsx)

> Hi Ava / Rishi,
>
> Thanks again for the demo last week — AtlasAI is excited to move to a dedicated deployment with Redwood....

From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Sofia Vega <svega@atlasai.com>
Date: Fri, 09 Mar 2028 12:08:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Cost/perf knobs before prod rollout - need owner map and checklist

Thanks for the owner breakdown, Ava. A couple of quick clarifying questions on quantization and evals so we scope effort correctly: 

1) Model variants: will AtlasAI be pinning to a specific model family (e.g., redwood-7b-qlora, redwood-13b-ft) for the initial rollout, or do you expect multiple variants? Quantization eval cost scales with the number of model variants.
2) Accuracy budget: what's the acceptable delta for our core NLU tasks? For example, is <1% hit rate drop acceptable on intent classification? That determines whether we do int8 vs int4 experiments.
3) Data privacy: any prompt-level masking or redaction required before we run quant evals on their samples?

If you can answer (1)–(3) in the kickoff, I'll prepare a short eval plan and a template for the accuracy/latency matrix.

—Rishi
Rishi Malhotra
Applied ML, Redwood Inference

> On Fri, Mar 9, 2028 at 11:03 AM Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Hi Sofia — thanks for the clear asks and for the survey....

From: Sofia Vega <svega@atlasai.com>
To: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 09 Mar 2028 13:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Cost/perf knobs before prod rollout - need owner map and checklist

Hi Rishi/Ava — quick answers inline: 

1) Model variants: we'll start with the redwood-13b-ft variant for the chat UI, but plan to evaluate the 7b family for low-cost burst paths. So please assume two variants initially.
2) Accuracy budget: for NLU intents we can accept up to 1.5% absolute drop; for content-generation quality we want manual review gates before promoting a quantized model to production.
3) Data privacy: prompts contain PII in some customer messages; we will provide a redacted sample set and an on-prem fork of logs for your evals behind the VPC.

I'll bring our infra lead (infra@atlasai.com) to the kickoff if we need to go deeper on VPC/KMS.

Thanks —
Sofia
(On Fri, Mar 9, 2028 at 12:08 PM Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com> wrote:)
> Thanks for the owner breakdown, Ava. A couple of quick clarifying questions on quantization and evals so we scope effort correctly...

Kickoff (Mon Mar 12, 11:00 AM PT) — Agenda: priorities, confirm pre-work, share log capture script
Checklist & owners (deliverable, due Wed Mar 14):
- Baseline capture script + sample logs (AtlasAI) — owner: AtlasAI infra
- Prefetch & cache TTL proposal (doc + expected hit-rate delta) — owner: Karthik Iyer (Redwood)
- Quant eval plan (int8/int4 matrix + eval metrics) — owner: Rishi Malhotra (Redwood)

Acceptance: we will only promote a quantized profile to canary after passing the accuracy gate (<1.5% intent drop) and manual QA for generative outputs. Expected economics: the owner map spreadsheet lists the rough cost savings (token cost %) per knob.

title: Indemnity: escalation triggers and evidence staging for IP claims
preview:
From: Jordan Mills <jordan.mills@prospectco.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rachel White <rachel.white@prospectco.com>
Date: Sat, 4 Mar 2028 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Potential IP scenario — how would Redwood handle 3rd-party claims?

Hi Aditya,

Thanks for the demo yesterday — momentum is good. Legal here flagged a hypothetical: ProspectCo embeds outputs from Redwood-powered inference in customer-facing features. If a third party alleges the output infringes their IP, what is Redwood willing to commit to for (a) defense and (b) indemnity?

Specifically:
- Who controls litigation (defend vs. reimburse)?
- What are timing/notice and evidence requirements (we would expect cooperation/hand-off of claim details)?
- Any exclusions (AI-generated content, open-source model issues)?

I attached a short scenario our counsel prepared. We need an initial position to proceed with procurement — timing is this week.

Appreciate a quick note on options and the playbook you typically use.

Jordan
Counsel, ProspectCo
Attachment: prospect_claim_scenario.pdf (1 page)


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jordan Mills <jordan.mills@prospectco.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, David Kim <david.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, 4 Mar 2028 10:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Potential IP scenario — how would Redwood handle 3rd-party claims?

Jordan — thanks, appreciate the concrete scenario. Looping in our legal and procurement leads (David, Kimberly) and Priya on the commercial side.

Short summary of commercial options we commonly offer:
1) Defense-Control (Redwood leads defense): Redwood conducts defense subject to cooperation from ProspectCo; Redwood retains control but will consult on settlement decisions. Typical cap = insurance limit + negotiated contract cap.
2) Reimburse (ProspectCo leads/controls): ProspectCo controls the defense and seeks reimbursement for reasonable costs; Redwood still requires notice and right to participate.
3) Hybrid (defend for certain claim types; reimburse for others): e.g., Redwood defends third-party patent/copyright claims tied to the model weights or infra; claims about customer prompts/inputs or downstream uses are reimburse-only.

Key practical asks we need from ProspectCo:
- Timely notice (we ask within 10 business days of learning of claim)
- Preservation of evidence and handoff of relevant prompts/outputs (we will reciprocally preserve logs up to agreed retention)
- Right to defend or nominate counsel if Redwood is to lead; cooperation clause if customer leads

David/Kim — can you propose concrete redline language and preferred caps/exclusions? Priya — if we land on hybrid, what commercial trade (discount/credit) do we offer?

I expect we can give ProspectCo an initial compromise by end of day if legal aligns.

Cheers,
Aditya
Director, BD — Redwood Inference


From: David Kim <david.kim@redwoodinference.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, 4 Mar 2028 13:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Potential IP scenario — how would Redwood handle 3rd-party claims?

Team — quick legal note / proposed redlines after reviewing ProspectCo's scenario: see attached redline snippet. Major points: 

1) Scope: Carve out \"AI-generated outputs created solely from customer prompts\" from Redwood's obligation to defend or indemnify. We can commit to defend and indemnify for third-party claims alleging infringement arising from Redwood-provided model code, weights, or underlying platform.

2) Notice and evidence staging: Require written notice within 10 business days; customer must provide prompt/output samples and any third-party complaint. Redwood to advise within 15 business days whether it will assume defense. If Redwood assumes defense, customer must provide a single privileged evidence package and a named contact for discovery — limit back-and-forth to preserve timelines.

3) Control/settlement: Redwood may assume defense with counsel approved by Redwood; settlements that require payment must be mutually agreed if they create ongoing obligations on the customer. If customer insists on controlling defense, Redwood will reimburse reasonable fees subject to review and the same evidence cooperation.

4) Caps / insurance: Recommend a combined approach — Redwood's indemnity limited to the lesser of (i) an order-form cap (e.g., $1,000,000) and (ii) Redwood's available insurance limit for the year. This preserves predictability.

5) Exclusions: No indemnity for claims arising from: (i) customer's modification of outputs after receipt, (ii) customer's failure to apply required mitigations, (iii) open-source dependencies for which customer took on risk per order form.

Attachment: redline_msa_indemnity_snippet.docx (2 pages) — includes proposed clause text and a short comment thread explaining tradeoffs.

If we want to soften scope for enterprise deals, we can trade additional assurances (e.g., expanded log retention, higher SLA credits) for a broader defense commitment. Thoughts?

-David
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jordan Mills <jordan.mills@prospectco.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, David Kim <david.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel White <rachel.white@prospectco.com>
Date: Sat, 4 Mar 2028 16:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Potential IP scenario — how would Redwood handle 3rd-party claims?

Jordan — thanks for the patience. Attached is a short summary of our proposed compromise and the redline snippet David prepared. High-level offer we can take to ProspectCo counsel: 

- Redwood will defend and indemnify for third-party claims that allege infringement by Redwood's platform (code, model weights, serving infra).
- Claims that appear to arise from customer-provided prompts, post-processing of outputs, or customer modifications are outside Redwood's indemnity; for those, Redwood will reimburse reasonable costs if the customer elects to control defense, subject to a review and mutual cooperation protocol.
- Notice within 10 business days; evidence handoff (single package) and Redwood to accept/decline defense within 15 business days.
- Cap: Redwood indemnity limited to the lesser of $1,000,000 or Redwood's available insurance limit.

If ProspectCo needs the carveouts narrowed for enterprise procurement, we're open to discussing concessions (longer log retention, SOC evidence package, incremental platform credits) in exchange for tightening the AI-output exclusion.

Can you confirm whether this is sufficient for your counsel to proceed? If yes, we can send a one-page summary + redline via DocuSign for signature and advance to final order-form language.

Best,
Aditya
Director, BD — Redwood Inference

> 
> 2) Notice and evidence staging: Require written notice within 10 business days; customer must provide prompt/output samples and any third-party complaint. Redwood to advise within 15 business days whether it will assume defense. If Redwood assumes defense, customer must provide a single privileged evidence package and a named contact for discovery — limit back-and-forth to preserve timelines.
>

"

title: Demo follow-up: proposed trial & success gates for Sandbar Tech
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Nguyen <alex.nguyen@sandbartech.com>, Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@sandbartech.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 28 Feb 2028 14:12:00 -0800
Subject: Demo follow-up: proposed trial & success gates for Sandbar Tech

Hi Alex / Elena —

Really appreciate you both taking the time today. Quick recap + proposed next steps so we keep momentum:

Highlights from the demo
- Retrieval and ranking flow matched your sample prompts; relevance looked strong against the test set you shared.
- End-to-end latency in the demo environment averaged ~180ms for single-turn QA (demo config).
- Built-in caching eliminated repeated token costs for exercise queries.

Proposed trial (6 weeks) — primary goals
1) Validate retrieval relevance at scale (50k queries over 6 weeks) with >80% pass rate on your acceptance prompt set.
2) Meet application latency target (we recommended 200ms p50; if you prefer we can relax to 300ms — see notes below).
3) Verify integration and security: SSO, allowlist, and audit-log delivery to your SIEM.

Evaluation plan & success criteria (high level)
- Acceptance prompt set: you provide 200 gold prompts + pass/fail rubric. Pass = top-3 contains correct answer per rubric. Target: 80%+ pass.
- Latency: p50/p95 targets for the production-like workload. Default suggestion: p50 < 200ms, p95 < 600ms.
- Cost baseline: we'll track token/unit cost during the trial and report unit economics per route.

Timeline (proposed)
Week 0 (kickoff): finalize scope, owners, and sign trial addendum
Week 1: connect SSO & allowlist; baseline ingestion of sample corpus
Weeks 2-5: ramp traffic for evaluation; weekly checkpoints with metrics
Week 6: final report + next-step recommendations (Dedicated/private sizing or hosted production rollout)

Owners
- Redwood: Ben Carter (sales lead), Marissa Cole (CS/eng owner), Jordan Blake (solutions/infra)
- Sandbar: Alex Nguyen (product), Elena Ruiz (IT/security) — please confirm any additional stakeholders (procurement, legal).

Next steps (asks for this week)
1) Confirm acceptance prompt set owner and target date for delivery.
2) Confirm whether latency target should stay at 200ms p50 or be relaxed to 300ms p50.
3) Sign the trial addendum (I attached a draft) or confirm procurement path.
4) Propose 60-90 minute kickoff slot week of Mar 12.

Attachments: proposed-trial-charter-v1.pdf (PDF, charter + metrics)
Recording: Sandbar-demo-recording.mp4 (30m) — link also in Drive: https://drive.redwood.com/s/abc123-trial-charter

Happy to run a quick technical deep-dive if Jordan or Marissa should join separately.

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0142


From: Alex Nguyen <alex.nguyen@sandbartech.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@sandbartech.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 01 Mar 2028 09:18:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Demo follow-up: proposed trial & success gates for Sandbar Tech

Thanks Ben — great demo yesterday. A few clarifying items from our side before we confirm kickoff:  

1) Latency: our product team is more comfortable with p50 < 300ms given our enrichment pipeline. Can Redwood operate at 300ms p50 while keeping relevance within the target? If there are trade-offs we should be explicit about them.

2) Data movement: we want to know what exact artifacts you need to ingest and any transformation steps. Will you accept a nightly S3 drop of the corpus?

3) Network / security: please send IP ranges and any firewall rules we need to allow. Our security team will also want to review your SOC 2 report.

4) Procurement: we can move quickly but need a sample SOW and confirmation whether a pilot PO can be used to start (we prefer a smaller pilot PO rather than a full MSA at kickoff).

If you can answer the above we can align procurement and schedule the kickoff.

— Alex
Alex Nguyen
Product Lead, Search
Sandbar Tech
alex.nguyen@sandbartech.com

Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 01 Mar 2028 11:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Demo follow-up: proposed trial & success gates for Sandbar Tech

On Wed, Mar 1, 2028 at 9:18 AM Alex Nguyen <alex.nguyen@sandbartech.com> wrote:
> Thanks Ben — great demo yesterday. A few clarifying items from our side before we confirm kickoff:  
> 
> 1) Latency: our product team is more comfortable with p50 < 300ms given our enrichment pipeline. Can Redwood operate at 300ms p50 while keeping relevance within the target? If there are trade-offs we should be explicit about them.
> 
> 2) Data movement: we want to know what exact artifacts you need to ingest and any transformation steps. Will you accept a nightly S3 drop of the corpus?
> 
> 3) Network / security: please send IP ranges and any firewall rules we need to allow. Our security team will also want to review your SOC 2 report.
> 
> 4) Procurement: we can move quickly but need a sample SOW and confirmation whether a pilot PO can be used to start (we prefer a smaller pilot PO rather than a full MSA at kickoff).

Thanks Alex — answers inline below: 

1) Latency: Yes — we can run the trial with p50 target of 300ms. Note: relaxing p50 increases the likelihood we use denser batching or larger effective batch windows to hit cost targets; relevance impact is typically negligible for retrieval-first flows but we’ll monitor drift. We’ll include both the 200ms and 300ms baselines in the weekly metric report so you can pick.

2) Data movement: nightly S3 drop is perfect. We need a manifest CSV and a sample document mapping (id, title, body, metadata fields). Jordan, can you own the detailed schema + ingestion checklist?

3) Network/security: Jordan will provide allowlist IPs and the minimal ports we require (443 outbound + health-check endpoints). Also: SOC 2 report and security questionnaire are attached via the Drive link.

4) Procurement: pilot PO is fine. We typically have a short trial addendum + pilot SOW (I attached an updated charter v1.1 with a procurement appendix). If Procurement needs a sample SOW, Marissa can share our standard pilot SOW template.

Attachments: proposed-trial-charter-v1.1.pdf (updated)
Drive: https://drive.redwood.com/s/abc123-trial-charter

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0142

title: Hosted API trial: lightweight quickstart & validation tasks
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sara Li <sara@finflow.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2028-03-02T15:12:00-07:00
Subject: Hosted API trial: lightweight quickstart & validation tasks

Hi Sara —

Thanks again for taking the demo today. Quick recap from our side and a proposal for a lightweight hosted trial to validate fit before considering Dedicated/VPC: 

1) Objective (2-week sandbox): validate latency & cost at your expected request profile and confirm output quality on representative prompts.
2) Deliverables we’ll provide: 
  - sandbox API keys + per-endpoint staging URLs
  - mapped endpoints for your three main surfaces (chat API, summarization pipeline, embedding endpoint)
  - a short prompt set + evaluation rubric we can run with you
3) Ops guardrails we propose for the trial: 
  - rate-slice: 50 RPS sustained, 150 RPS burst window (configurable)
  - token/response size cap guidance (we’ll recommend limits per endpoint)
  - prefix/KV caching enabled for chat to save costs on repeated contexts

To move forward we need: a sample traffic CSV (small, anonymized), 1–2 example payloads, and a target SLA window for latency percentile (p95). I attached a one-page quickstart plan and a short playbook so your engineers have a checklist.

Proposed next steps:
  - you share sample_traffic_template.csv populated with a day of traffic (anonymized)
  - we provision sandbox access (48h turnaround)
  - 30-min technical preflight call (optional)

If you have strict residency or legal requirements that force a Private deployment, we can map that path — but my recommendation for fastest validation is Hosted so we can iterate quickly.

Attachments: quickstart_plan.pdf, hosted_quickstart_playbook.pdf

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Enterprise Solutions 
Redwood Inference

Date: 2028-03-02T17:20:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Hosted API trial: lightweight quickstart & validation tasks

Sara —

Quick answers to your items: 

1) Region/residency: yes — we can provision sandbox endpoints in the EU region for the trial. For production, we support region pinning on Hosted and Dedicated; Private/VPC is of course on-prem or customer region.

2) Dedicated ramp: operationally we can provision a Dedicated pool after an initial validation — typically a 2–6 week lead time depending on capacity and contract. We'll scope that after the trial if you want to proceed.

3) Rate limits: the 50 RPS sustained is a starting slice to protect shared pools. We can open a higher temporary slice for a short validation window (e.g., 200 RPS for 2 hours) if you want to observe behavior under load. For production we'll work with your SREs to define autoscaling and committed throughput.

If it helps, I attached a short one-pager comparing Hosted vs Dedicated so you can share with procurement/infra. Also proposing a 30-min technical working session with Tom + your infra to finalize the sandbox mapping — are you available tomorrow morning or Friday afternoon PT?

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>

Cc: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwoodinference.com>

Subject: Next steps: capacity sizing window + technical demo

Hi Sam —

Thanks again for the time earlier — great to get a clear view on the primary flows and the latency tail constraints for your payments reconciliation pipeline. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we can lock a dedicated sizing session: 

Recap from discovery:
- Primary feature: real-time reconciliation + anomaly detection on incoming transactions (avg request ~120 tokens, spike to ~800 tokens)
- Target latency: p95 end-to-end inference <= 200ms for single-request paths; batch async OK for occasional bulk jobs
- Expected concurrency: 30–70 simultaneous active sessions during peak window

Goal for the dedicated sizing call:
- Validate throughput profile (qps, concurrency), pin expected token lengths, and run a short load planning exercise so we can recommend GPU pool size and batching settings
- Walk through a short technical demo showing our KV cache and batching behavior for similar workloads

Proposed 60-min slots (all PT) — which works for you?
- Tue Mar 6: 10:00–11:00 PT
- Wed Mar 7: 14:00–15:00 PT (preferred for our infra engineer)
- Thu Mar 8: 08:00–09:00 PT

Attendees we plan to join from Redwood: myself (Solutions), Irene Choi (Platform Engineer), optional: Mina Haddad (Security) if you want to discuss retention/ingress details. From your side please include the infra owner + any compliance contacts.

Before the call: I attached a short capacity intake template (see attachment below) — if you can fill the highlighted cells (avg/95th token length, expected sustained qps, burst qps, SLAs) that’ll make the session much higher fidelity.

Attachments: [Attachment: capacity-intake-template.xlsx]

If Wed 14:00–15:00 PT works, we’ll send a calendar invite and a short pre-call checklist. If none of these work, send 2–3 alternative windows and we’ll accommodate.
