title: Post-call: NimbleCart throughput & streaming snapshot — next steps
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Emily Zhang <emily@nimblecart.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 21 Feb 2028 15:12:00 -0800
Subject: Post-call recap + traffic/profile asks (throughput & streaming)

Thanks for taking the time today — quick recap from our discovery so we can size and propose the right pilot. Please see bullets and the specific asks at the bottom.

What we heard on the call:
- Primary product: real-time order recommendations surfaced during checkout.
- 60% of requests are streaming responses (progressive recommendations) vs 40% full-response.
- Target e2e SLO: 95th percentile start-of-stream < 300ms, full-response 95th < 700ms for median payloads.
- Typical request: 12 tokens prompt, ~70 tokens average response, but peak flows can have 200+ tokens/resp.
- Traffic pattern: steady baseline during day with short bursts (15–45s) during promotions. Peak windows observed ~10x steady QPS.

Key unknowns we need to size correctly (please share if available):
1) 48–72h sample of request logs (timestamp, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, streaming flag) OR a CSV export (see example attached).
2) Peak QPS and steady-state QPS by hour/day (if you have 95th/99th percentiles for windows that helps).
3) Typical concurrency per user/session, and throttling behavior in-app (max concurrent open streams).
4) Any strict bandwidth/region constraints (we heard US-only but confirm).

Our initial hypotheses (to validate):
- Steady-state: ~150–300 QPS (depending on region split).
- Burst peak: ~1,200–2,000 QPS for 30–90s during promotions.
- Avg tokens/sec baseline: nominal; token-based batching will meaningfully change cost at peak.

Next steps / asks:
- Emily: share the 48–72h CSV sample and any recent peak windows (attach to HubSpot / Drive).
- Jordan + Marcus: we’ll run a quick sizing sweep once we have the sample and return a short proposal (hosted vs dedicated recommendation with rough cost & attendees for pilot).
- Goal: draft a pilot plan and SLA note by Thu (48 hours) so NimbleCart can review with infra/procurement.

Attachments: sample CSV template attached (nimble_example_traffic.csv).

Thanks — Ava
Ava Chen
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ava@redwood.com

---
(Attachment: nimble_example_traffic.csv — CSV stub, expected columns: timestamp, request_id, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, streaming_bool, region)

From: Emily Zhang <emily@nimblecart.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: NimbleCart Infra <infra@nimblecart.com>
Date: Tue, 21 Feb 2028 17:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + traffic/profile asks (throughput & streaming)

Thanks Ava — great recap. Attaching a 72h export and a short SLA doc for context (nimble_sla_requirements.pdf). High level answers inline: 

- Steady-state QPS (US region): ~220 QPS (weekday daytime).
- Observed promotional peaks: 1,800–2,100 QPS for ~30–60s windows; happens ~3–5x/day during promotions.
- Token distribution: median response ~68 tokens, 75th ~120 tokens, occasional long tails to 350 tokens. Prompt tokens mostly 8–20.
- Streaming: ~60% of calls are streaming; client opens up to 6 concurrent streams per user during heavy checkout sessions.
- Regional constraint: US primary, EU negligible for now.

We attached nimble_example_traffic.csv (48h sample: includes a 45s peak window). Let us know if the columns/format don’t work. We also want an initial cost ballpark and whether you think Hosted API will meet the SLOs or if we should plan Dedicated.

Emily Zhang
Head of Infra, Product
NimbleCart
emily@nimblecart.com

(Attachment: nimble_example_traffic.csv — 48h sample, includes a 45s peak window at 2028-02-19 14:22:xx UTC)

From: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>
To: Emily Zhang <emily@nimblecart.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 22 Feb 2028 08:09:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + traffic/profile asks (throughput & streaming)

Appreciate the data, Emily — dug into the CSV quickly and here are initial sizing notes (high-level, conservative):

Observations from sample:
- Steady: ~210 QPS, median response ~68 tokens, streaming fraction ~58%.
- Peak window (45s): sustained ~1,950 QPS mapped to many short-lived sessions.

Initial sizing guidance (to budget/propose pilot):
- Hosted (fast time-to-value): can likely meet the SLOs for steady-state with appropriate batching and streaming optimizations. For peaks, Hosted with auto-burst may work but risk of throttling depending on other tenants — recommend a committed Dedicated lane for predictable peak handling.
- Suggested pilot footprint: 2 x dedicated GPU nodes for steady-state (handles ~250 QPS comfortably with batching + cache). Add burst pool sizing: 6–8 nodes or equivalent autoscale to handle 1,800–2,000 QPS with target 95th start-of-stream < 300ms. Exact node type TBD based on quantization profile and kernel mix.
- Token throughput: at 70 tokens/resp, peak token/sec at 2,000 QPS ≈ 140k tokens/sec — this drives batching and memory/KV cache needs.
- Concurrency: client opens up to 6 streams/user — we need to model concurrent KV cache pressure and connection limits.

Short asks from our side to finalize numbers:
1) Can you confirm any hard SLA on cost vs latency for promotions? (procurement input).
2) Provide a 1-hour high-resolution log around a representative peak (preferably raw request-level timestamps) so we can run a burst simulation.

Jordan will draft a 2-page pilot proposal (hosted vs dedicated tradeoff, estimated cost range, success criteria) and share by EOD. Ava / Marcus will pick the proposal owners.

-Jordan
Jordan Blake
Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference

Date: Tue, 21 Feb 2028 16:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Redwood demo — calendar shows "No meeting link"

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the invite — our team was able to add the event to Outlook, but the calendar entry shows "No meeting link available" and the body has no conference URL. It looks like the event was forwarded through our internal assistant and Outlook removed the conferencing metadata (we see an empty conference-line where a Meet/Zoom link should be).

Can you resend the invite or just drop the direct meeting URL in an email so we can attach it to the event? We want to avoid everyone joining the wrong meeting room.

Short background: we use Exchange 2019 + an internal automation that proxies forwarded invites; the automation strips attributes it doesn't recognize.

Thanks — Alicia

Alicia.Gomez@clearwave.com

title: Log egress options and purge guarantees for EU tenant — FinServCorp
preview:
From: Andrew Miller <andrew.miller@finservcorp.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>
Cc: Legal Ops <legal-ops@finservcorp.com>
Date: Sat, 18 Feb 2028 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Log egress and purge guarantees for our EU tenant

Hi Mateo,

We're preparing for an audit and our legal team needs clear answers on two points for our EU tenant:

1) Can Redwood export both access logs and audit logs on demand in a tenant-scoped, per-tenant NDJSON or CSV format? If so, what destinations are supported (S3/GCS/Azure Blob, SFTP)?

2) Once Redwood deletes tenant data (for retention or at our request), can you provide a signed attestation and a manifest of the exported records? Also need to confirm: what exact retention windows apply to audit vs access logs in EU (including any cold backups or snapshots you maintain), and how long they are kept before being wiped from backups?

We expect PHI-adjacent metadata in some logs — legal wants to be sure you do not retain any customer-provided PII in long-term backups beyond the retention window.

Attached a short list of evidence items we're expecting as part of the audit (audit-export-manifest.csv).

Thanks — appreciate a rundown and any playbook / export runbook you can share.

Andrew
Andrew Miller
Head of IT Compliance
FinServCorp

[Attachment: audit-export-manifest.csv (CSV)]

From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>
To: Andrew Miller <andrew.miller@finservcorp.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 20 Feb 2028 08:23:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Log egress and purge guarantees for our EU tenant

Hi Andrew — thanks for the clear list. Sharing a concise summary up-front, then looping in Rishi and Marissa (infra + product) for details and runbook links:

Summary:
- Export formats: tenant-scoped exports available in NDJSON or CSV. Supported destinations: Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, Azure Blob Storage, and secure SFTP endpoints. Exports are encrypted in transit (TLS 1.3) and at rest using customer-managed keys if requested.
- Retention: default audit-log retention (hosted) is 90 days; access logs (request-level traces) default 30 days. For Enterprise (contracted) tenants we offer customizable retention up to 2 years for audit logs and 1 year for access logs via Dedicated/Private plans.
- Backups & snapshots: snapshots for disaster recovery are retained for a rolling 14-day window in EU-region-only deployments. We do not keep long-term backups of tenant log content beyond configured retention unless under legal hold.
- Deletion & attestation: we provide an export manifest and deletion attestation for tenant-initiated erasure. For legal holds, we mark records and suspend scheduled deletion until hold removal.

I'll let Rishi confirm the technical export cadence and the exact fields we include by default (for PII minimization), and Marissa will add the compliance playbook link and SLA references.

Next steps: Rishi — can you confirm the default field set, whether we persist raw request bodies in audit logs for EU tenants, and the exact snapshots/backups policy? Marissa — please attach the export playbook and SLA table.

Best,
Mateo Alvarez
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

> On Sat, Feb 18, 2028 at 9:12 AM Andrew Miller <andrew.miller@finservcorp.com> wrote:
> > We're preparing for an audit and our legal team needs clear answers on two points for our EU tenant: ...
> > [Attachment: audit-export-manifest.csv (CSV)]


From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Andrew Miller <andrew.miller@finservcorp.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 21 Feb 2028 10:15:00 -0500
Subject: Re: Log egress and purge guarantees for our EU tenant

Hi all — technical clarifications below. Keeping it focused so Andrew's legal team can map items to their checklist.

1) Export payload / fields:
- Audit logs: include timestamp, tenant_id, actor_id (redacted if requested), operation, resource_path, result_code, request_id, server_region. We exclude request/response bodies from audit logs by default in EU-hosted tenants. That minimizes PHI/PII surface.
- Access logs (request traces): include timestamp, tenant_id, route, latency_ms, node_id, and a hashed client_ip (salted, reversible on request for sanctioned investigations). We do not persist plaintext client IPs in long-term exports unless customer explicitly requests them and signs a Data Processing Amendment.

2) Export mechanisms:
- Ad-hoc exports: initiated via Console (Export -> Tenant snapshot) or API. Exports generate a signed manifest and are pushed to the target destination you select.
- Scheduled exports: available (daily/weekly/monthly) for Enterprise customers. Exports are chunked into time-bounded NDJSON files (max 4GB per chunk).
- Format: NDJSON preferred for streaming/ingest; CSV available for smaller slices.

3) Retention & backups:
- Hosted EU tenants: audit logs default 90 days, access logs 30 days.
- Enterprise Dedicated/Private: configurable up to audit=24 months, access=12 months via contract.
- Backups/snapshots: short-lived DR snapshots (14 days) in-region; cold backups are not used for tenant-level logs. We do not export tenant logs to internal long-term archival systems.

4) Deletion flow & attestation:
- Standard deletion: once retention lapses or customer requests erasure, records are removed from primary store and removed from snapshot caches during next snapshot rotation.
- Attestation: we produce a signed deletion statement (PDF) and include an export manifest listing file checksums and object keys as evidence.
- Legal holds: process: Legal Ops attaches a hold; we suspend deletions, mark records in the index; exportable as-is. Removing a hold requires documented approval from Legal Ops.

5) Edge cases & compliance notes:
- If a customer requires that no reversible hashes exist for client IPs, we can salt-and-drop reversible fields and only keep non-reversible hashes.
- For cross-border concerns: EU-region hosted tenants keep all log content in EU by default. Exports to non-EU destinations require a customer-signed data transfer addendum.

Attachments: I'm attaching the internal export options doc and the SLA extract for export delivery SLAs.

Rishi Malhotra
Principal Infra Engineer, Redwood Inference

[Attachment: redwood-log-export-options.pdf (PDF)]
[Attachment: sla-export-table.pdf (PDF)]

> On Mon, Feb 20, 2028 at 8:23 AM Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Summary: Export formats: tenant-scoped exports available in NDJSON or CSV...

Subject: Handoff protocols & resourcing scenario for Sable Therapeutics private VPC pilot

Team / Mark / Elaine —

Thanks for the sync last week. Sable has confirmed they want a short pilot (6 weeks) in our EU region to validate the VPC/private-control-plane model before deciding on Dedicated capacity. They asked for a minimal-handoff protocol and a clear staffing scenario that shows who needs to be involved at each milestone (network setup, image validation, SOC evidence exchange, day-1 runbook).

I attached a first-pass ops_runbook_outline.pdf and staffing_scenarios.xlsx — please treat these as draft. My asks:
- Karthik: can you validate the network handshake steps and call out any nonstandard infra we need?
- Jonas: ownership for the control plane handoff (runbooks, monitoring, incident playbooks).
- Naomi: procurement/contracting checklist items to start (exceptions/PO timeline).
- Ben: CS/solutions lead for day-1 onboarding and acceptance tests.

Mark / Elaine — appreciate confirmation of the pilot scope below and any constraints we should account for (on-prem airgap? specific KMS/HSM?). If you can upload the Sable requirements doc I will reconcile with our ops outline.

Objective: produce a 1-page handoff protocol + a single staffing scenario with primary/backup owners by Friday (2028-02-24). If that timeline is tight, flag now and I'll push the ask.

Thanks,
Aisha

Aisha — quick technical notes from my side. I sketched these into staffing_scenarios.xlsx under \"Network/Infra assumptions\" but calling out here as well: 

1) Connectivity model: Sable confirmed they want our standard VPC-peering path with private control plane (no internet egress for model traffic). If they require an air-gapped option we will need extra images and an offline sync process (adds ~2w to pilot).
2) KMS integration: if they want BYOK with their HSM, we need the KMS hooks enabled on the control plane side and a short security review. Timeline estimate: +1 week for config + SOC evidence update.
3) Image validation: we will need a small validation cluster (1 GPU) in their region to smoke-test model startup and KV cache behavior; Ops can run this but will need a dedicated engineer for the initial 72h window.

Open questions for Sable: which KMS provider, and are there any corporate IP allowlist constraints?

title: Enclave sealing, egress assertions, and attack scenarios — RFI response
preview:
From: LunarHealth Security Team <security@lunarhealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Maya Singh <maya@lunarhealth.com>
Date: Mon, 20 Feb 2028 08:10:00 -0500
Subject: RFI: Enclave sealing, egress assertions, and high-level attack scenarios

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the quick intro last week. LunarHealth has started compiling a vendor RFI focused on deployment separation and egress assurance for inference workloads. We need concise, verifiable answers we can include in our procurement packet. High level asks: 

- A short description of your enclave sealing/tenant isolation model for hosted inference (network + runtime boundaries).
- How you assert/verify egress controls (allowed destinations, ephemeral bastions, audit logs).
- A one-page attacker scenario matrix: how a compromised tenant pod could or could not reach other tenants, control plane, or sensitive corp network.
- Any evidence artifacts you can share (network diagrams, egress logs, design doc excerpts).

Can you provide an initial written response and attachments today/tomorrow? We may follow up with a short walkthrough call.

Thanks,
LunarHealth Security Team
security@lunarhealth.com

--
Maya Singh
Sr. Security Engineer, LunarHealth


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: LunarHealth Security Team <security@lunarhealth.com>
Cc: Maya Singh <maya@lunarhealth.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 20 Feb 2028 11:05:22 -0500
Subject: Re: RFI: Enclave sealing, egress assertions, and high-level attack scenarios

Maya — thanks for the clear list. Attached is our concise RFI packet (Enclave_Sealing_Overview.pdf) and a simplified network diagram (Network_Diagram_v3.svg). High-level answers below; I kept them short so your team can paste into the packet. Happy to expand any item.

1) Enclave sealing / tenant isolation model (summary)
- Runtime: tenants are pinned to isolated compute pools (Dedicated or isolated VPC subnets) with per-tenant process namespaces and mandatory sidecar proxies that mediate all inbound/outbound inference traffic.
- Networking: each tenant is assigned a dedicated virtual network segment (VLAN-like overlay) and egress policy; control-plane traffic flows over a mutually authenticated mTLS channel to a customer-specific controlplane endpoint.
- Data-at-rest: model weights are stored in per-tenant storage namespaces and encrypted with a tenant-scoped KMS key; KV caches are namespaced and inaccessible across tenants.

2) Egress assertions and verification
- Allowlist-first egress: default-deny model for all outbound flows. Operator-configured allowlists (CIDR / FQDN) per-tenant.
- Bastionless access: we provide ephemeral, jumpless upload endpoints for model artifacts; operator-only admin tasks use HSM-backed signed tokens.
- Evidence: per-request egress logs (timestamped, src-tenant, dst-ip/dst-fqdn, subject model id) retained per the contract length; sample logs included in egress-proof-logs-2028-02.zip.

3) Attacker scenario matrix (very short)
- Compromised tenant inference process: can access only that tenant's KV and model artifacts; sidecar enforces egress allowlist and blocks lateral RPCs.
- Compromised sidecar: sidecar is sandboxed; a compromised sidecar cannot read KMS-wrapped keys (HSM keys require control-plane approval).
- Compromised control-plane operator credential: certificate rotation + SIEM alerts and multi-admin approval for KMS operations (exfiltration risk mitigated by KMS audit logs).
- Network-level compromise of underlying host: hypervisor/host hardening, minimal host services exposed, and host-level egress monitoring with forced tenant tagging in logs.

Attachments: Enclave_Sealing_Overview.pdf (3 pages), Network_Diagram_v3.svg (simplified), egress-proof-logs-2028-02.zip (redacted samples).

If it helps, I can schedule a 30-min walkthrough with our security architect (Laura) and Aditya from infra. Availability attached as a link in the diagram folder.

Regards,
Ben Carter
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0123



From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 20 Feb 2028 13:02:11 -0500
Subject: Re: RFI: Enclave sealing, egress assertions, and high-level attack scenarios (internal review)

Ben — quick internal note before we send the packet to LunarHealth. A couple of optional clarifications they may ask for and we should be consistent on: 

- HSM/KMS: confirm whether we keep customer-managed KMS (bring-your-own-key) for Private deployments vs. Redwood-managed KMS for Hosted. Suggest we explicitly call out both paths in the packet.
- Bastionless wording: clarify that operator tasks that require shell access are performed via audited jump-hosts for Dedicated, but Private on-prem can use customer-managed bastions.
- Egress evidence retention: what retention window do we offer for Hosted trial vs. enterprise SLA? Need a concrete number in the packet.

Aditya — can you provide the default retention settings we typically offer (hosted dev vs enterprise reserved)? Also please confirm if our proof logs include DNS-resolved FQDNs (we do but confirm).

Laura
Laura Bennett
Head of Security Solutions, Redwood Inference
laura.bennett@redwood.ai


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: LunarHealth Security Team <security@lunarhealth.com>
Cc: Maya Singh <maya@lunarhealth.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 20 Feb 2028 14:40:03 -0500
Subject: Fwd: RFI: Enclave sealing, egress assertions, and high-level attack scenarios — evidence + clarifications

FYI — forwarding our internal clarifications along with updated packet. See embedded clarifications and explicit retention numbers below. Forwarded attachments: Enclave_Sealing_Overview.pdf, Network_Diagram_v3.svg, egress-proof-logs-2028-02.zip.

---------- Forwarded message ----------
From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 20 Feb 2028 13:02:11 -0500
Subject: Re: RFI: Enclave sealing, egress assertions, and high-level attack scenarios (internal review)
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>

(see internal clarifications)

Additional clarifications (finalized for packet):
- KMS: Hosted = Redwood-managed tenant-scoped KMS (AES-256 keys wrapped with an HSM-root; per-tenant key IDs included in audit logs). Private (VPC/on-prem) = customer-managed KMS supported; we provide integration guides.
- Bastion/access: Dedicated = operator access via ephemeral jump-hosts with session recording; Private = customer controls bastion topology per their policy.
- Egress retention: Hosted standard = 30 days; Enterprise SLA = configurable up to 365 days (contract). All logs include DNS-resolved FQDNs and reverse lookups where available; network artifacts show tenant tag and model_id.

--

Ben (continued): If these answers work for you, I can attach the packet as a single PDF and we can book a 30-min walkthrough. For transparency, I included a link to the diagram folder: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3cENCLAVE-SECURE.

Regards,
Ben



From: Maya Singh <maya@lunarhealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: LunarHealth Security Team <security@lunarhealth.com>, Tom Hargreaves <tomh@secassure.io>
Date: Tue, 21 Feb 2028 15:45:00 -05:00
Subject: Re: RFI: Enclave sealing, egress assertions, and high-level attack scenarios

Ben / Laura / Aditya — thanks for the thorough packet and the sample logs. Useful, and the clarifications on KMS and retention answer the bulk of our checklist. A couple quick asks before we finalize: 

- Can you confirm whether your Hosted offering provides certificate transparency/auditing for control-plane cert changes?
- Would you be able to do a 30-min walkthrough this week (our security ops + one of our infra engineers + Tom from SecAssure). Our windows: Wed 17:00–18:30 ET or Thu 09:00–11:00 ET.

If you can share the single PDF packet and a meeting invite, we can mark this RFI as 'complete' on our side.

Thanks,
Maya Singh
Sr. Security Engineer, LunarHealth
security@lunarhealth.com

--
Tom Hargreaves <tomh@secassure.io>
Senior Consultant, SecAssure

title: Re: production latency & error surge — logs / audit handling questions
preview:
From: Jordan Clark <jordan.clark@apexpay.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>; ops@apexpay.com
Cc: Priya Singh <psingh@apexpay.com>
Date: Mon, 20 Feb 2028 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: production latency spike and 502s — urgent

Alex / Redwood team —

We started seeing a severe latency spike and a wave of 502s for our payments verification route around 08:45 PT. This is impacting live authorizations. Attached is a short CSV snapshot of request latencies and error counts from our side.

Two quick asks:
- Can you confirm what runtime logs you can pull for the timeframe 08:40–09:00 PT?
- Do your logs include request payloads or any customer-submitted sensitive fields? Our security team needs immediate clarity before we escalate further.

We have an executive call in 30 minutes — any quick answers appreciated.

—Jordan
VP Engineering, ApexPayments
[attachment: apexpay_latency_snapshot_20280220.csv (CSV, 48KB)]

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Jordan Clark <jordan.clark@apexpay.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Priya Singh <psingh@apexpay.com>
Date: Mon, 20 Feb 2028 09:22:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: production latency spike and 502s — urgent

Jordan —

Thanks for the heads-up and the CSV. We’ve opened an incident (INC-20280220-APX) and Marcus is on lead for the runtime traces. Quick answers up front:
- Yes, we can export request-level runtime traces for the timeframe you specified.
- By default our runtime traces do NOT persist customer payloads in plaintext. We record request metadata (route, model variant, token counts, latency, error codes) and limited hashed identifiers for correlation.

However, depending on your configuration (webhook body passthrough, custom functions) there are a few places where upstream payloads may have been forwarded into logs. We will do a scoped extraction and provide:
  1) a hashed/filtered trace export for 08:40–09:00 PT (no raw inputs)
  2) a redacted export if legal requires payloads for the audit — we’ll apply field-level redaction.

Marcus / Vanessa — please pull the trace export and a short timeline. Priya — please let us know any legal constraints from Apex on retention or redaction.

I’ll stay on the line during your exec call if helpful.

—Alex
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
Redwood Inference — Production Systems
[internal note: incident INC-20280220-APX opened; priority=P1]

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Clark <jordan.clark@apexpay.com>, Priya Singh <psingh@apexpay.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 20 Feb 2028 10:05:12 -08:00
Subject: Re: production latency spike and 502s — runtime trace and extraction

Team —

I pulled the runtime trace for 08:38–09:02 PT and started a filtered export. Summary of what I see:
- Spike origin: sustained token generation latency on model-root that caused upstream timeouts at our edge proxies.
- Error surface: 502 gateway errors, correlated with queueing delays in the GPU pool for the model variant pinned by Apex.
- Logged fields: request id, timestamp, route, model variant, seq_len, latency_ms, error_code, and a hashed request fingerprint.

What I DID NOT see in the runtime trace: raw request bodies or un-hashed user inputs. There is a separate <function-logs> stream for customer function outputs that can include payloads if customers opt-in; I checked Apex’s config and there’s no global opt-in flag set.

Attachments for your review:
- redwood_runtime_trace_20280220.json (filtered export) — contains no raw payloads, only metadata and hashed fingerprints.
- support_thread_screenshot.png — console view at time of spike.

Next steps I recommend:
1) Share the filtered export with Apex security/legal.
2) If Apex requests raw payloads for an audit, we need an explicit written request from Apex Legal (psingh@apexpay.com) specifying fields and lawful basis; we will then perform a controlled redaction export and log chain-of-custody.
3) Preserve the raw short-term buffer under legal hold while we validate config — I’ve toggled export protection on the incident so traces are immutable for 30 days.

—Marcus
Principal Reliability Engineer
[attachment: redwood_runtime_trace_20280220.json (JSON, 2.1MB)]

From: Priya Singh <psingh@apexpay.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 20 Feb 2028 11:03:45 -08:00
Subject: Re: production latency spike and 502s — legal / evidence requirements

Thanks — that clarifies a lot. ApexPayments legal requests the following:
1) A timeboxed export (08:40–09:00 PT) that includes headers and metadata. Do not include raw cardholder or full personal data unless absolutely required; if you assert none exists, please state that in writing.
2) If any customer-submitted fields are present in the function logs, provide a field-level mapping and a redacted sample.
3) A short attestation that the exported artifact was pulled from immutable traces and the chain-of-custody (who pulled it, when, and how it was stored/transferred).

—Priya Singh

- Yes, we can export request-level runtime traces for the timeframe you specified.
- By default our runtime traces do NOT persist customer payloads in plaintext. We record request metadata (route, model variant, token counts, latency, error codes) and limited hashed identifiers for correlation.

However, depending on your configuration (webhook body passthrough, custom functions) there are a few places where upstream payloads may have been forwarded into logs. We will do a scoped extraction and provide:

- Spike origin: sustained token generation latency on model-root that caused upstream timeouts at our edge proxies.
- Error surface: 502 gateway errors, correlated with queueing delays in the GPU pool for the model variant pinned by Apex.

What I DID NOT see in the runtime trace: raw request bodies or un-hashed user inputs. There is a separate <function-logs> stream for customer function outputs that can include payloads if customers opt-in; I checked Apex’s config and there’s no global opt-in flag set.

- redwood_runtime_trace_20280220.json (filtered export) — contains no raw payloads, only metadata and hashed fingerprints.

3) Preserve the raw short-term buffer under legal hold while we validate config — I’ve toggled export protection on the incident so traces are immutable for 30 days.

Hi Sarah / Tim,

- For greater auditability we can provide a signed attest statement (timestamped) from our Platform Ops team; let us know if you want Redwood's attestation on letterhead.

To keep momentum, we put together a proposed sequencing for evidence delivery so your procurement and security reviewers can get the highest-risk controls first. Attached is a control_mapping.xlsx with our suggested ordering and requested artifact types (attestation, log sample, configuration screenshot, or full export).

- Can you confirm which controls must have raw artifacts vs. an engineer attestation?

- Day 4–7: Infrastructure and change management artifacts
- Day 8–14: Remaining controls + remediations / clarifications

title: Clarify productization & go-to-market usage limits (order form redlines)
preview:
From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>
To: Claire Huang <claire@clearwave.io>, Sam Ortega <sam.procure@clearwave.io>, ClearWave Legal <legal@clearwave.io>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Feb 2028 09:17:00 -0800
Subject: Clarify productization & go-to-market usage limits (order form redlines)

Hi Claire / Sam / ClearWave Legal,

Thanks for taking a look at the order form redlines. I attached v2 (with a short redline summary) — the two items that keep popping up on our side are: 1) productization / downstream use of model outputs, and 2) training/data use consent.

Quick summary of our position:
- We are comfortable granting customers broad internal productization rights (embedding the model into internal features) but want to limit third-party redistribution or standalone resale of generated outputs unless explicitly licensed.
- For model training / feedback: by default we request a limited feedback license to improve the hosted model; customers may opt-out to prevent any training on their content.

Questions for you (so Legal can draft clear redlines):
1) Do you need the prohibition on third-party resale to be spelled as an exclusive clause, or is a non-resale restriction sufficient?
2) Will ClearWave require explicit telemetry/retention evidence (logs, access controls) before agreeing to the limited feedback license? If so, which artifacts are needed?
3) Any preferred fallback language around marketing/go-to-market uses (e.g., attribution, co-marketing, use of customer logos vs. specific product outputs)?

Proposed next step: if you confirm specifics on Q1–Q3, Laura (cc'd) will draft targeted order form language and Ben will confirm commercial levers (one-time fee, usage tiers) for productization rights.

Thanks — really want to get to a short, actionable order form that both sides can sign.

Best,
Avery Johnson
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
avery_johnson@redwood.com
(415) 555-0187

Attachments: proposed_orderform_redlines_v2.docx (redlines), redline_summary_table.xlsx (one-page matrix)

From: Claire Huang <claire@clearwave.io>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>, ClearWave Legal <legal@clearwave.io>, Sam Ortega <sam.procure@clearwave.io>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 15 Feb 2028 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Clarify productization & go-to-market usage limits (order form redlines)

Avery — thanks for the clear summary and the attachments. A few clarifications from our side:

1) ClearWave is comfortable with internal productization but we need explicit protection against third-party resale of model outputs. Language we prefer: “Customer may not redistribute or resell Model outputs to third parties for use as a stand-alone product.” Is that compatible with Redwood's standard playbook?

2) For training/feedback: we will only grant a feedback license if Redwood provides evidence of data segregation, telemetry retention policy, and the ability to opt-out at any time. Specifically, procurement needs:
- A short evidence pack (PDF) confirming retention windows and access controls (who can access raw customer prompts/outputs).
- Confirmation that any training that uses customer data will be subject to an opt-in process and will be limited to non-identifying aggregates unless explicitly agreed.

3) Marketing/go-to-market: we can agree to attribution (e.g., "Built with Redwood Inference for ClearWave") but we cannot allow Redwood to use customer-generated outputs as promotional content without explicit sign-off per-instance.

If you can incorporate the three points above into the next draft, we can route back to ClearWave legal for a final pass. Also cc'ing our procurement lead Sam to track the procurement timeline.

Best,
Claire Huang
Sr. Product Manager, ClearWave
claire@clearwave.io

--- Original message ---
> From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>
> Sent: Tue, 14 Feb 2028 09:17:00 -0800
> Subject: Clarify productization & go-to-market usage limits (order form redlines)



From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 15 Feb 2028 11:25:00 -0800
Subject: Fwd: Clarify productization & go-to-market usage limits (internal)

Forwarding Claire's reply — highlights below.

Internal note / ask:
- Claire wants a hard prohibition on resale/redistribution unless separately licensed. She suggested the specific sentence “Customer may not redistribute or resell Model outputs to third parties for use as a stand‑alone product.”
- For training: ClearWave needs an "evidence pack" for retention/telemetry plus an opt-in for any training.
- Marketing: attribution ok, but no using customer outputs in Redwood marketing without per-instance sign-off.

My recommended compromise to speed signature:
1) Order form clause: allow internal productization (explicitly called out) + prohibit redistribution/resale to third parties (non-exclusive restriction). Add an exceptions row for "partner integrations" that require a separate addendum.
2) Training: offer default limited feedback license for model improvement with an explicit opt-out exercised by account owner. Provide an evidence pack (short PDF) that documents retention windows and access controls (we can pull from our Telemetry team).
3) Marketing: allow attribution language and anonymized case studies with written approval — require per-instance sign-off for any output used verbatim in marketing.

Ben — can we add a commercial option (one-time productization fee or higher committed usage tier) if they want broader downstream resale rights? If so, please suggest language/fee band.

Laura — could you draft two short order-form alternatives: (A) standard internal usage + non-resale prohibition + limited feedback license with opt-out; (B) expanded downstream use (includes resale) subject to a negotiated commercial addendum. I can send either to Claire depending on commercial direction.

Thanks,
Avery

Attachment: redline_summary_table.xlsx

From: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
To: Claire Huang <claire@clearwave.io>, ClearWave Legal <legal@clearwave.io>, Sam Ortega <sam.procure@clearwave.io>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 16 Feb 2028 15:42:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Clarify productization & go-to-market usage limits (order form redlines)

Claire and ClearWave Legal — thanks for the detailed feedback. Attached are the two short form options we discussed ("Option A" and "Option B") and a one-page evidence pack covering telemetry, retention, and access controls. Summary below (high level):

Option A (recommended, faster path):
- Customer is granted internal productization rights to use generated outputs inside Customer products and services for internal operations and for resale as part of Customer products only if explicitly licensed.
- Prohibition: "Customer may not redistribute or resell Model outputs to third parties as a stand-alone product or service without a separate written license from Redwood."
- Training / Feedback: Redwood may use anonymized/aggregated customer feedback to improve the Hosted Service unless Customer opts out via the account owner. Evidence pack provided.
- Marketing: Redwood may reference Customer relationship and provide anonymized examples with prior written approval.

Option B (commercial alternative):
- Customer may obtain broader downstream rights (including limited resale) subject to a separate commercial addendum and a one-time productization fee or higher committed spending level.

Next steps we propose:
1) Please review Option A language in the attached docx. If ClearWave is comfortable, we can issue a DocuSign for the order form with Option A and the standard MSA.
2) If ClearWave prefers Option B, we can provide the commercial terms (fee tiers) for internal approval — Ben can own that.

I've attached: proposed_orderform_redlines_v2.docx (with both options tracked), redline_summary_table.xlsx (matrix mapping concerns to language), and evidence-ip-proof.pdf (telemetry & retention summary).

If acceptable, please confirm which option (A or B) and we'll circulate the finalized order form via DocuSign by end of week.

Regards,
Laura Bennett
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference
laura_bennett@redwood.com

--
Avery Johnson | Account Executive | Redwood Inference
Ben Carter | Director, Commercial Ops | Redwood Inference

preview:
From: Audit Team (ComplianceInc) <audit@complianceinc.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
Cc: John Patel <john.patel@acmehealth.com>
Date: 2028-02-10T08:02:00-08:00
Subject: Request: personnel verification artifacts for SIG/CAIQ review

Hi Camila,

Thanks for taking this on. As part of our SIG intake for Acme Health we need a short transmittal of personnel evidence covering hiring background checks, annual security training completion, and the onboarding/offboarding checklist chain of custody. Ideally we get: 1) redacted BG check samples (3), 2) a training completion export with timestamps, and 3) a signed onboarding checklist for a recent starter and a recent leaver.

Please include the dates and the redaction method you used (PII removal/hashing). If any items contain third-party vendor attestations (background vendor reports), flag that in the transmittal.

Target turnaround: EOD Feb 13 if possible.

Appreciate it —
Rajiv (Audit Team)
ComplianceInc

--
Attachment requested: list_of_required_artifacts.txt

From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
To: Audit Team (ComplianceInc) <audit@complianceinc.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>, John Patel <john.patel@acmehealth.com>
Date: 2028-02-10T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Request: personnel verification artifacts for SIG/CAIQ review

Hi Rajiv —

Thanks for the clear list. Marcus and I will assemble a minimal, audited transmittal with redactions and a notes sheet describing the redaction approach. A few quick notes:

- Background checks: we will provide 3 redacted PDFs using our standard redaction pipeline (names hashed, SSNs redacted, dates retained at month/year granularity).
- Training completions: we will export the Learning Management System (LMS) report with user email, training module, completion timestamp, and the certificate id.
- Onboarding checklist: we will include a signed checklist for a hire in Jan 2028 and a leaver from Dec 2027; chain-of-custody notes will show who uploaded and who redacted.

I will put the artifacts into a shared Drive folder and provide DocuSign proof for the signed checklist. Marcus will add the redaction log as a CSV. Expect initial package by end of day Feb 13; if we can deliver earlier we will.

Thanks,
Camila
Manager, Trust & Compliance
Redwood Inference

--
Attachments planned: redacted_background_checks.pdf; training_completion_matrix.csv; onboarding_checklist_signed.pdf

From: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>, Audit Team (ComplianceInc) <audit@complianceinc.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>, John Patel <john.patel@acmehealth.com>
Date: 2028-02-12T11:47:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Request: personnel verification artifacts for SIG/CAIQ review

Looping in details on the redaction process and file inventory.

Inventory (preliminary):
- redacted_background_checks.pdf (3 samples; filenames: bg_redacted_1.pdf, bg_redacted_2.pdf, bg_redacted_3.pdf)
- training_completion_matrix.csv (columns: email, module, completed_at, cert_id)
- onboarding_checklist_signed.pdf (signed by hiring manager; DocuSign envelope id attached)
- redaction_log.csv (who performed redaction + timestamp)

Redaction method: automated pipeline (Python/WASM) that masks PII fields and replaces identifiers with deterministic hashes (sha256 truncated). SSNs are removed; DOB preserved as month/year. We retained internal user ids for traceability but those are salted and stored in the audit ledger (not in the shared artifacts).

If ComplianceInc needs vendor-signed attestations for the background vendor, I can request the vendor letter but it will take 24–48 hours.

-Marcus

title: Private deployment: onramp certification, VPC peering & appliance acceptance sprint
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Dana Liu <dana.liu@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Tom Reynolds <tom.reynolds@acmehealth.com>
Date: Tue, 07 Feb 2028 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Private deployment: onramp certification, VPC peering & appliance acceptance sprint

Hi Dana —

Thanks again for the call this morning. Per our conversation, I'm putting together the checklist and a short ops sprint we can run the week of 02/14 to finish VPC peering, private endpoint setup, and appliance acceptance testing. High-level asks from your side:

- Confirm one or two CIDR blocks we can use for the VPC peering (we recommended a /20 or larger).
- Provide a CPE contact and a time window for the initial ingress test (we typically need a 2-hour window for full KV cache warm-up and end-to-end smoke tests).
- Share the customer network inventory (attached CSV stub) and the IP allowlist for private endpoints.

I've attached a short network inventory template and our MVP checklist (acme_mvp_network_checklist.xlsx) — these are the fields we need filled to generate the on-prem appliance manifest and certs.

Proposed sprint milestones:
1) Preflight & inventory completed (we get your filled CSV) — target 2/10

title: SLO ops: proposed measurement method & sample exchange
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alyssa Park <alyssa@fleetly.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 02 Feb 2028 15:10:00 -0800
Subject: SLO ops: proposed measurement method & sample exchange

Hi Alyssa — great to meet earlier. Quick recap from our discovery and a concrete proposal so we can align on a reproducible benchmark run. TL;DR: please share a ~50-entry prompt sample (non-PII) and confirm the target SLOs below so we can schedule a short qualification run next week.

Recap of what we heard from your team:
- Primary workload: short-chat assistant for logistics (avg response length ~40 tokens).
- Typical peak: bursty spikes from 50 -> 200 concurrent users during route-commit windows.
- Important SLOs: low tail latency for button-driven UX and streaming token start for progressive render.

Our proposed measurement spec (open for tweak):
- Payload: 32-token completion target (avg); canonical prompt set (you attach).
- Latency SLOs (measured at service edge, end-to-end): p50 <= 120ms, p95 <= 450ms for 32-token completions; stream token latency <= 50ms between tokens after first-token.
- Concurrency/throughput: sustained 150 concurrent streams, peak 250 concurrent for 5-minute bursts.
- Cold vs warm runs: report both cold (no KV/prefix cache) and warmed (after 5k requests) with separate tables.
- Reproducibility: fixed model version, pinned quantization, deterministic prompt ordering, and seeded RNG for sampling where applicable.

Measurement methodology notes we intend to run on our side:
1) Harness: single-script runner that replays prompts with exact pacing; timestamp each request/response at the edge and at the model runtime.
2) Warmup: optional warmup phase of 5 minutes, then three 10-minute steady-state windows.
3) Streaming: measure first-byte latency, per-token spacing, and flush interval.
4) Error handling: log retries separately and include a short error taxonomy.

Attachments: I attached a short method proposal and an example prompt CSV to demonstrate the expected format. Please redact any PII and replace real user ids with stable placeholders.

Immediate asks for Fleetly:
- Send a prompt sample (CSV) with 40–80 examples, labeled by scenario (e.g., route-change, ETA-check).
- Confirm whether the SLO targets above map to your expectations or propose alternate numbers.
- Identify a contact for procurement/PO (cc'ed procure@ if applicable).

If you’re good with the above, we can run a controlled qualification in a dedicated window and share the run artifacts (raw logs, per-request timings, and a runbook to reproduce). Proposed run window: next Wed–Thu (Feb 8–9), 09:00–13:00 PT.

Thanks — Ben

--
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0178
[Attachment: redwood-method-proposal.md (text/markdown)]
[Attachment: fleetly-prompt-sample.csv (text/csv)]

From: Alyssa Park <alyssa@fleetly.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Fleetly Procurement <procure@fleetly.com>
Date: Thu, 02 Feb 2028 17:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: SLO ops: proposed measurement method & sample exchange

Thanks Ben — this is helpful and matches our internal priorities. I attached a 60-row prompt sample and a short scenario mapping. A couple of clarifying points from our side:

- Our UX team is most sensitive to p95, so if tradeoffs are needed prioritize tail latency even if it increases cost by ~10–15%.
- For streaming: we need first-token to appear within 180ms on average and token cadence to be smooth (no >150ms gaps) for the first 8 tokens.
- We can provide a procurement contact (see cc).

A few questions:
1) For the cold vs warm runs, what does 'cold' mean operationally? Zero cache across the whole cluster or per-model warmup?
2) For determinism, are you able to pin sampling seeds for the model variant you plan to use? We rely on repeatable outputs for downstream QA.

I attached the CSV (fleetly-prompt-sample.csv). Let us know if the format needs changes.

Best,
Alyssa Park
Head of Product, Fleetly Labs
alyssa@fleetly.com
[Attachment: fleetly-prompt-sample.csv (text/csv)]

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Alyssa Park <alyssa@fleetly.com>
Date: Fri, 03 Feb 2028 08:42:00 -0800
Subject: Re: SLO ops: proposed measurement method & sample exchange

Alyssa — thanks for the prompt pack. I reviewed and attached a short checklist of measurement definitions so our runs report apples-to-apples numbers. Key clarifications and our reproducibility checklist: 

Measurement definitions (canonical):
- Request latency: wall-clock from client request arrival at Redwood edge to final token emitted to client (exclude network RTT if client-side instrumentation not provided).
- First-token latency: time from edge arrival to first byte of model output reaching the edge.
- Per-token spacing: median and 90th percentile inter-token interval measured at model runtime.
- Error rate: any HTTP 5xx or model-run timeout (include a small table breaking out failure class).

Reproducibility checklist (what we will deliver and what we expect from Fleetly):
- We will: publish the exact model image (hash), quantization flag, and commit id used for the run; provide the harness script with pinned request pacing; record raw traces and anonymized request payloads.
- Fleetly: provide the sanitized prompt CSV (done), confirm which prompts should be considered high-priority (label), and acknowledge the run window.
- Warmup semantics: our "cold" run will start with an empty KV/prefix cache for that model on the dedicated serving pool; warmed runs will be after a 5k-request ramp.
- Determinism: we can pin sampling seeds for greedy/deterministic modes; for sampling-heavy prompts we will include seed and temperature in the artifact, and we recommend running both deterministic and sampled variants.

Operational asks:
- Decision: prioritize tail latency (Alyssa's note). We will schedule one baseline (cost-optimized) run and one tail-priority run (higher resources) so you can compare tradeoffs.
- Can Fleetly confirm a QoS escalation contact for the run (email/phone)?

I attached a short "measurement-defs.md" that we will include in the run artifacts. Once Ben locks a window I will spin up the dedicated pool and share the infra hash.

—Marissa
Marissa Cole
Performance Solutions Lead, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com | +1 (650) 555-0112
[Attachment: redwood-method-proposal.md (text/markdown)]

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alyssa Park <alyssa@fleetly.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Fleetly Procurement <procure@fleetly.com>
Date: Fri, 03 Feb 2028 09:23:00 -0800
Subject: Re: SLO ops: proposed measurement method & sample exchange

Perfect — thanks Alyssa/Marissa. Quick next steps and owners: 

1) Fleetly: confirm run window (we proposed Feb 8–9 09:00–13:00 PT) and share procurement PO if you want a dedicated reserved slot (procure@). Owner: Alyssa.
2) Redwood infra: Marissa will provision a dedicated pool and share the infra hash + model image id. Owner: Marissa.
3) Redwood test run: Karthik will run an initial smoke test (small sample) 24h prior and share results. Owner: Karthik.
4) Fleetly QA: please confirm which 10 prompts are highest-priority (label them in the CSV) so we can include targeted debug traces. Owner: Alyssa.

Artifacts we'll deliver after the run: raw per-request timings (CSV), model image hash, harness script, and a short readout (SLO table, tail analysis, cost delta for tail-priority run). We will also provide a single reproducer script and instructions so your engineering team can replay the run.

If that all looks good I’ll mark the HubSpot deal and book the run window. Any constraints on the proposed dates?

Thanks — Ben

--
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
[Link: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/12345/deal/FL-2028-Q1-09]

Date: Tue, 17 Jan 2028 15:02:00 -0800
Subject: Velora Health — post-demo fit & model-delta next steps

Thanks again for taking the demo today — really helpful session. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we stay aligned: 

- Findings from demo: Velora's triage assistant performs well on surface-level clarification prompts but we saw inconsistent extraction on diagnostic fields (ICD code suggestions and confidence).
- Root cause hypothesis: recent model variant we served to your trial (rw-medium-v3) shows a shift on medical-entity recall vs rw-medium-v2 we originally benchmarked.

Action items I propose (please confirm):
1) Engineering will snapshot the current trial traffic and pin traffic to rw-medium-v2 for the next 48–72 hours so you have stable outputs for your QA. (Requested: engineers Priya + Dev)
2) We'll share the benchmark CSV and the prompt set used in the demo for your QA team to run locally. (attached: velora_benchmarks_v2.csv, prompt_set_v1.txt)
3) We'll run a focused A/B eval (50/50) between v2 and v3 on a 2k-golden-set and report token-level diffs and false-negative ICD extractions. Deliverable: diff report + recommendation. (owner: Marissa)
4) If the diff exceeds the agreed thresholds (see suggested thresholds in the Drive link), we will either pin rw-medium-v2 for your account or configure on-request rollback with a 1-click pin guard.

Please let me know if Sam or Lucy want the pin to happen immediately, or if you prefer we run the A/B first and then decide. I recommend pinning now to give your clinical reviewers a stable baseline while we investigate.

Thanks — Alex

--
Alex Martinez

Pinned the account routing for Velora Health to rw-medium-v2 as requested. Details below: 

- Snapshot ID: snap-velora-20280117-01
- Route ID: route-velora-001
- Pin type: account-wide temporary (expires in 72h unless extended)
- A/B harness: A/B job queued (job id: ab-velora-20280118) will run tomorrow at 02:00 UTC against the 2k-golden-set uploaded to the Velora Drive folder.

Notes on what I observed during a quick replay of the failing cases: the v3 variant introduced a tokenization change in our tokenizer config (subword merge threshold) that caused slightly different entity splits for some ICD codes. That explains the recall gap. Will include per-case tokenization diffs in the report.

If you want an immediate rollback instead of a pin, I can do a force-rollback to v2 (imperative); pin preserves the previous variant for auditing.

Date: Thu, 19 Jan 2028 09:45:00 -08:00

Quick update on the A/B: preliminary diff (first 500 cases) shows a 4.1% drop in ICD recall for rw-medium-v3 vs v2, and a 0.9% increase in hallucination tokens measured by our QA heuristics. Full 2k report is uploaded to Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1VeloraBenchmarks (file: velora_diff_report_20280118.pdf).

Recommendation: keep the account pinned to rw-medium-v2 until we ship a targeted tokenizer/decoding patch for the v3 line. I propose the following timeline: 
- 19 Jan morning: keep pin in place (Priya/Dev to extend pin expiration to 14 days for stability)
- 20–22 Jan: smallship tokenizer patch and internal canary on rw-medium-v3-fix
- 23 Jan: if internal canary is green, run targeted 1k regression against Velora golden set and then unpin if parity met

Sam/Lucy — can you confirm you want the 14-day pin extension? If yes, Priya/Dev will extend this afternoon and we'll treat this as the official mitigation.

Thanks — Marissa

title: Action required: failed auth pattern — proposed invoice split & temporary payment fallback
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Anika Rao <anika@solaredge.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 15 Jan 2028 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Action required: failed auth pattern — proposed invoice split & temporary payment fallback

Hi Anika,

Quick update from our side after the batch of declined captures on 2028-01-14 (see attached retry log). We see repeated declines for the planned annual invoice (INV-202801) — most responses from the acquirer are "do_not_honor" or soft-decline with 3DS timeout.

To avoid a large auth attempt that could push the account to a chargeback or automatic suspension, we propose the following immediate plan if you approve: 

1) Split the outstanding balance into two invoices: INV-202801-A (smaller amount to clear current usage) and INV-202801-B (remainder with 7-day retry window).
2) If you can provision a temporary virtual card (or let us accept a one-time card number via our secure payment link), we'll attempt a single-authorize for INV-202801-A and then retry INV-202801-B with a staggered schedule to avoid repeated large authorizations.
3) Provide the legal VAT entity that should be billed for INV-202801-A vs INV-202801-B — we can issue the first invoice to your UK entity (VAT: GB123456789) and the second to your Irish entity if that matches your accounting. If the VAT mapping differs, please confirm the correct VAT registration for each invoice.

Attachments: invoice-SolarEdge-202801.pdf (pro forma), payment-retry-log-20280115.csv (server-side retry details).

Can you confirm by EOD today whether: (a) procurement can accept split invoices, and (b) whether you can provision a temporary payment instrument? If helpful, we can also route a Stripe escalation.

I cc'ed our billing and payments ops so we can move quickly once you confirm.

Thanks —
Ben

--
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales Manager | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com | +1 (415) 555-0192
Redwood Inference


From: Anika Rao <anika@solaredge.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: procurement@solaredge.com
Date: Mon, 15 Jan 2028 17:48:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Action required: failed auth pattern — proposed invoice split & temporary payment fallback

Ben —

Thanks for flagging this. We did see the declines on our end. Procurement is okay in principle with splitting the invoice, but I need two things before I can sign off: 

- Electronic evidence of the VAT entity mapping for each invoice (we need the invoice to show the VAT reg no. that matches our internal GL).
- Confirmation that splitting the invoice will not change the payment terms or trigger a different tax treatment.

Our card team also confirmed that our current corporate card is hitting a 3DS timeout for large single-authorization flows — that’s why the soft-declines are showing. I can ask them to issue a single-use virtual card, but that will require procurement approval (looped in).

Also please do not suspend access during this — we rely on the integration for a scheduled launch this week. If splitting is the fastest path, please proceed and send updated docs to procurement@solaredge.com.

Best,
Anika
Head of Partnerships
SolarEdge

Subject: Escalation request — repeated do_not_honor / 3DS timeouts for SolarEdge (DEAL-SEL-2027-042) — case prep attached

Hi Stripe team,

We're requesting an urgent escalation for our merchant SolarEdge (customer id: sel_94721) — multiple capture attempts on 2028-01-14 returned "do_not_honor" and 3DS timeouts. This is causing a potential suspension risk and could lead to a chargeback if captures are retried aggressively.

Requested actions: 
- Please review case #87921 and advise if an alternate acquirer path can be used for two small captures.
- If alternate acquirer is not possible, request an extended retry window and guidance on best-practice card rotation / one-time virtual card acceptance to reduce decline odds.
- Please confirm whether stripe can flag these attempts to reduce false-positive chargeback disputes.

Included: payment-retry-log-20280115.csv (server-side attempts), invoice-SolarEdge-202801.pdf (pro forma). We have the last4 of one failed attempt (ending 4242) and are prepared to share additional merchant evidence on request.

Thanks for the quick help — we need to finalize with procurement by EOD tomorrow to avoid service impact during SolarEdge's launch.

Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales Manager, Redwood Inference

- Option B: If you prefer to accept a temporary virtual card, we advise a single-authorize & capture for INV-202801-A and then retry INV-202801-B after 72 hours.

We've attached a brief escalation report (stripe-case-87921.pdf) and created a support ticket link for SolarEdge: https://dashboard.stripe.com/support/cases/87921. Please confirm which option you want us to execute and provide the last4 for any card you want prioritized for retry. 

Also: to reduce chargeback risk, ensure descriptors match "Redwood Inference" and include a billing contact on the invoice (procurement@solaredge.com). If you need us to hold off any automated retries from our side while you split invoices, reply and we will pause capture attempts for 24 hours.

Subject: Escalation request — repeated do_not_honor / 3DS timeouts for SolarEdge (DEAL-SEL-2027-042) — case prep attached

Hi Stripe team,

We're requesting an urgent escalation for our merchant SolarEdge (customer id: sel_94721) — multiple capture attempts on 2028-01-14 returned "do_not_honor" and 3DS timeouts. This is causing a potential suspension risk and could lead to a chargeback if captures are retried aggressively.

Requested actions: 
- Please review case #87921 and advise if an alternate acquirer path can be used for two small captures.
- If alternate acquirer is not possible, request an extended retry window and guidance on best-practice card rotation / one-time virtual card acceptance to reduce decline odds.
- Please confirm whether stripe can flag these attempts to reduce false-positive chargeback disputes.

title: Greenline Health: elevated P99 latency & increased 5xx after model flip
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 15 Jan 2028 22:05:00 -0800
Subject: Greenline Health: elevated P99 latency & increased 5xx after model flip

Team —

Greenline's telemetry spiked right after we flipped to the quantized v2 candidate in prod at 21:30 PT. Highlights:
- P99 latency increased from ~210ms to ~620ms for text-generation route.
- 5xx rate rose from 0.2% to 1.8% (mostly OOM-ish kernel failures and a few 502s).
- Throughput dropped on their dedicated pool; queue depth went up.

I attached a short CSV with p95/p99 buckets for the 30m before/after. Can infra/runtime confirm whether this looks like a kernel selection regression or a capacity mis-balance (too many hot shards on same node)?

Immediate asks:
1) Karthik — can we rollback the canary and re-route Greenline to v1? If a rollback is quick, we should do it and notify the customer.
2) Marissa — loop in Customer Success and prep a brief exec-level note for Alice/Dan. They want visible ownership.
3) Evelyn — flag if you think we should spin up extra burst capacity on the Dedicated pool for now (we can cover cost if needed).

Attachments: p99_telemetry_20280115.csv (30MB stub), SLA_playbook_Greenline.pdf

Ben Carter
Director, Customer Reliability
Redwood Inference


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 15 Jan 2028 22:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Greenline Health: elevated P99 latency & increased 5xx after model flip

On Mon, Jan 15, 2028 at 10:05 PM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Team —
> 
> Greenline's telemetry spiked right after we flipped to the quantized v2 candidate in prod at 21:30 PT.

Thanks for the heads-up, Ben. Quick triage notes:
- I see an uptick in GPU memory allocations around the same timestamp in the dedicated pool logs; a few tasks spilled to host and triggered soft OOM handlers.
- The new kernel path for the quantized attention looks like it increases temp memory for short sequences on a subset of model shards. That explains both the latency and 5xx trends.

Recommended immediate actions (order matters):
1) Revert Greenline's routing to v1 (canary -> rollback). This is a per-customer routing change and should be <5min.
2) If rollback isn't clean, enable their cross-region fallback to our eu-west standby (temporary routing policy).
3) We'll collect a memory heap dump from the affected nodes and push a hotfix to kernel selection logic.

I'll start the rollback and log steps in PD-76890. Ben/Marissa, let me know when you want me to hit the rollback button.

Karthik
Sr. Engineering Manager, Serving Runtime


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Alice Kim <alice.kim@greenlinehealth.com>, Dan Ortiz <dan.ortiz@greenlinehealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 15 Jan 2028 22:40:00 -0800
Subject: RE: Greenline Health: elevated P99 latency & increased 5xx after model flip

Alice / Dan —

Thanks for the flag. We're treating this with high priority. Quick status:
- Root cause (prelim): a model runtime kernel path introduced higher transient memory use on the quantized variant we flipped for you.
- Immediate remediation: we are proposing to roll you back to the previous model variant (v1) while the runtime team issues a targeted fix.
- Expected customer impact if we rollback: transient ~3-5 minute routing switch; performance should return to baseline after rollback.

Planned cadence:
- 22:50 PT: rollback starts (we will send a short "rollback started" email).
- 23:00 PT: progress update and confirmation once baseline is restored.
- Tomorrow 09:00 PT: joint post-mortem and next steps with engineering and your SREs.

We're also offering to credit one week of overage-delta in recognition of the disruption. I'll confirm that with Ben/Evelyn and follow up.

If you'd prefer a voice bridge, we can spin up a 30-min exec bridge right now; otherwise we'll proceed with the rollback and push updates in-channel.

—Marissa
Principal Customer Success Manager
Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 15 Jan 2028 22:46:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Greenline Health: elevated P99 latency & increased 5xx after model flip

Do the rollback now. Marissa, offer the exec bridge and the one-week credit; Evelyn if you approve we can convert that to a formal credit on the invoice (chargeback to Redwood for this incident). I'll own the customer note and exec alignment.

Ben


From: Alice Kim <alice.kim@greenlinehealth.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Dan Ortiz <dan.ortiz@greenlinehealth.com>
Date: Mon, 15 Jan 2028 23:01:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Greenline Health: elevated P99 latency & increased 5xx after model flip

Thanks, Marissa and Ben.

Please proceed with the rollback. We appreciate the credit offer and would like a 30-minute bridge after confirmation so our SREs can review logs with your team. We'll keep our bridge open at 23:15 PT.

—Alice
VP Engineering, Greenline Health


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alice Kim <alice.kim@greenlinehealth.com>, Dan Ortiz <dan.ortiz@greenlinehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 17 Jan 2028 09:42:00 -08:00
Subject: Greenline incident follow-up: stabilized, next steps, and credit confirmation

Alice / Dan —

Follow-up from our bridge and remediation timeline:
- Action taken: rollback to v1 completed 2028-01-15T22:54:00 PT. Baseline performance restored; p99 back to ~220ms, 5xx reduced to 0.25%.
- Engineering next steps: (a) runtime team to land kernel selection guard, (b) extra integration test for quantized variants on dedicated pools. Estimated delivery: 48–72 hours.
- Compensation: Evelyn approved a one-week credit to offset the incident. Marissa will send formal credit paperwork to your procurement inbox by EOD.

Attachments: SLA_playbook_Greenline.pdf (for reference).

Thanks for your partnership — we know this was disruptive. I'll keep the post-mortem summary and timeline up-to-date in HubSpot (link below) and we'll follow up with a joint retrospective next week.

Ben Carter
Director, Customer Reliability
Redwood Inference

title: MSA redline: interim access & emergency relief (TileVan)
preview:
From: Elena Morales <elena.morales@tilevan.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Mark Rivers <mark.rivers@tilevan.com>
Date: 2028-01-14T09:22:00-08:00
Subject: TileVan - redlines re: governing law & injunctive relief

Hi Ava,

Attached is TileVan's redline of the MSA (TileVan_Draft_MSA_redline.docx). Our legal team is taking a firm position on two points that are non-negotiable for our board:

1) Governing law: TileVan requires New York law to govern the agreement.
2) No mandatory arbitration as to claims for injunctive relief — our counsel needs an express carve so we can seek emergency relief in state or federal court in New York.

We are happy to consider arbitration for ordinary disputes, but we need a carveout so that we can preserve the ability to obtain temporary and injunctive relief quickly for IP and data protection issues.

Please confirm whether Redwood can accept New York law for this account or propose an acceptable compromise.

Also attaching a short cover note from our procurement team describing the timeline: they need an executed MSA by 01/26 to meet a launch window.

Thanks,
Elena Morales
Senior Counsel, TileVan
Attachments: TileVan_Draft_MSA_redline.docx


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Elena Morales <elena.morales@tilevan.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-01-14T11:08:00-08:00
Subject: Re: TileVan - redlines re: governing law & injunctive relief

Elena — thanks for the redline and the timeline. We're excited to get TileVan onboard. A few quick thoughts from our side:

- Governing law: Redwood typically asks for Delaware law for corporate predictability and consistency across our contracts. If TileVan insists on New York, we'd like to propose a middle-ground: Delaware law with consent to interim injunctive relief in New York courts (i.e., venue for emergency relief only).

- Arbitration / injunctive carve: We can accept a narrowly tailored carveout that permits either party to seek temporary injunctive relief in court (to preserve evidence or stop immediate harm), provided the carveout is limited to: (a) preservation/forensic actions, (b) immediate stop-the-bleed measures, and (c) not a free-for-all for merits disputes. We would expect those proceedings to be limited in scope and subject to an expedited meet-and-confer protocol.

I've attached a short proposal (RDW-eta-emergency-carve-proposal.docx) with draft language that aims to preserve TileVan's commercial need for quick emergency relief while keeping arbitration as the primary mechanism for final disputes.

If that compromise is acceptable, we can flag limited edits and circulate a clean copy for signatures. If TileVan cannot accept Delaware at all, please confirm and we can escalate internally.

Best,
Ava Chen
Head of Sales, Redwood Inference
Redwood Inference
Attachments: RDW-eta-emergency-carve-proposal.docx


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: 2028-01-15T09:34:00-08:00
Subject: internal: suggested redline language and escalation plan

Team — following Ava's reply to TileVan, I reviewed their redline and our proposed carve. Key points / suggested edits: 

1) Narrow the injunctive carve to "emergency injunctive relief necessary to preserve confidential data, prevent exfiltration, or immediately enjoin use of infringing materials." Avoid broad language like "any equitable relief."

2) Add a 24-hour notice + mandatory teleconference requirement before filing for emergency relief unless notice would frustrate the relief (i.e., ex parte permitted where necessary). This shows good-faith attempt to avoid surprises and is often acceptable to enterprise customers.

3) Limit expedited discovery tied to the injunctive motion: emergency disclosure only as to the threatened harm, with a 7-day production timeline and tightly scoped custodians. 

4) If TileVan flatly refuses Delaware law, propose Delaware law but allow choice of New York for interim relief proceedings (venue-only carve). That preserves Redwood precedents while respecting their preference for NY courts in emergencies.

5) Commercial fallback: if they remain immovable on both governing law and an expansive injunctive carve, we should consider an escalation to the CRO for a deal exception (the launch timing is attractive, but legal exposure needs guardrails).

I'll draft exact contract language for the carveout and the procedural guardrails; Ava, do you want me to send that directly to Elena or should we loop Karthik to soften commercial framing?

Kimberly Park
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference


From: Elena Morales <elena.morales@tilevan.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Mark Rivers <mark.rivers@tilevan.com>, procurement@tilevan.com
Date: 2028-01-16T16:03:00-08:00
Subject: Re: TileVan - redlines re: governing law & injunctive relief

Ava / Kimberly — thanks for the quick turn. We reviewed RDW-eta-emergency-carve-proposal.docx with our GC. The compromise is acceptable in principle; the board will only sign off if the carve language explicitly permits emergency relief in New York courts and does not require preliminary arbitration filings before seeking an injunction.

We are comfortable with Delaware law as the governing law for the contract body if the emergency-relief venue is New York and the carve is worded to allow ex parte relief where necessary. One small edit requested: preserve the right to seek interim relief for alleged misappropriation of confidential information without requiring a 24-hour meet-and-confer where notice would prejudice the relief.

Attached is a corrected redline that accepts your edits except for that one change (TileVan_Accepted_Redline.docx). If Redwood can accept that narrowly tailored exception on notice, TileVan can proceed to signature and procurement will issue the PO within 48 hours after countersignature.

Please advise next steps — if you accept, we'll route the countersignature through DocuSign and expedite the PO.

Regards,
Elena Morales
Senior Counsel, TileVan
Attachments: TileVan_Accepted_Redline.docx

title: Sponsor intro & exec brief — slot realignment + evening option
preview:
